question: Q4
title: Encryption Key Files Served Without Authentication via /encryptionkeys/:file
data_flow: GET /encryptionkeys/:file → params.file → path.resolve('encryptionkeys/', file) → res.sendFile(). No authentication middleware is applied (server.ts line 1651: app.use('/encryptionkeys/:file', serveKeyFiles())). The only traversal prevention is blocking '/' characters — no check for URL-encoded variants or '..' sequences.
exploit_scenario: Unauthenticated attacker requests GET /encryptionkeys/jwt.pub and receives the RSA public key. Combined with the hardcoded RSA private key in insecurity.ts (lines 1193), the attacker can forge valid JWT tokens for any user including admin. Additionally, requests for /encryptionkeys/jwt.pem or other key material succeed without any login.
framework_protection: None. The /encryptionkeys route is registered before any authentication middleware. The directory is also browseable via serveIndex (server.ts line 1650).
fix_recommendation: Add security.isAuthorized() and an admin role check before serveKeyFiles(). At minimum, remove directory listing for encryptionkeys and restrict access to authenticated administrators only.

question: Q1
title: No Rate Limiting or Account Lockout on Login Endpoint
exact_code: app.post('/rest/user/login', login())
data_flow: POST /rest/user/login -> login() handler in routes/login.ts -> models.sequelize.query() with raw SQL. No middleware applies rate limiting or lockout before this handler in server.ts. Only /rest/user/reset-password and /rest/2fa/* endpoints have rateLimit() applied.
exploit_scenario: An attacker can issue unlimited login attempts against /rest/user/login with no throttling. A credential-stuffing or brute-force script can iterate common passwords against known email addresses (e.g., admin@juice-sh.op) indefinitely without triggering any block or delay.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Apply express-rate-limit middleware specifically to POST /rest/user/login with a low window (e.g., 5 requests per 15 minutes per IP). Additionally implement account lockout after N consecutive failures by recording failed attempts in the DB and temporarily setting isActive=false.

question: Q2
title: Passwords Hashed with MD5 (No Salt)
exact_code: export const hash = (data: string) => crypto.createHash('md5').update(data).digest('hex')
data_flow: insecurity.hash() is called from models/user.ts password setter (UserModel set password hook), from routes/changePassword.ts for current-password verification, and from routes/2fa.ts setup/disable for password confirmation. All password storage and comparison uses MD5.
exploit_scenario: MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsalted. An attacker who obtains the Users DB table (e.g., via SQL injection on the login endpoint) can crack all passwords instantly using precomputed rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated hashcat. The admin password 'admin123' would be cracked in milliseconds.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Replace crypto.createHash('md5') with bcrypt (cost factor >= 12) or argon2id. Update the hash() function and all call sites. Add a migration to re-hash passwords on next login. Ensure salts are unique per user.

question: Q3
title: RSA Private Key Hardcoded in Source Code for JWT Signing
exact_code: const privateKey = '-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----\r\...'
data_flow: privateKey is a module-level constant in insecurity.ts. It is used by jwt.sign() in authorize() to sign all JWTs issued at login and during 2FA flows. The public key is read from a file (encryptionkeys/jwt.pub) and that directory is publicly browsable via /encryptionkeys route in server.ts.
exploit_scenario: The private key is embedded in source code and committed to the repository. Any developer, contractor, or attacker who reads the source (e.g., via GitHub leak or the exposed /encryptionkeys endpoint) can forge arbitrary JWTs for any user, including admin. Additionally, the /encryptionkeys directory is served publicly with directory listing enabled, leaking jwt.pub to unauthenticated users.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Remove the hardcoded private key. Load it at runtime from an environment variable or secrets manager (e.g., AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit private keys to source control. Remove the public /encryptionkeys static file server route, or at minimum require authentication.

question: Q1,Q2,Q3
title: jsonwebtoken 0.4.0 - Critically Outdated with Known CVEs (Current: 9.x)
exact_code: "jsonwebtoken": "0.4.0"
data_flow: JWT tokens created/verified via jsonwebtoken 0.4.0 → used throughout auth middleware (express-jwt) → bearer tokens in Authorization headers → user identity/session for all protected routes
exploit_scenario: jsonwebtoken 0.4.0 is 10+ major versions behind current 9.x. CVE-2022-23529: object injection via secretOrPublicKey. CVE-2022-23540: accepts 'none' algorithm allowing unsigned tokens. CVE-2022-23541: algorithm confusion attacks (RS256 vs HS256 key confusion). Attacker forges arbitrary JWT tokens claiming admin identity by passing alg:none in the header, bypassing signature verification entirely.
framework_protection: None — algorithm enforcement was only added in jsonwebtoken 6.x. Version 0.4.0 predates all security hardening. The alg:none bypass is trivially exploitable.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to jsonwebtoken ^9.0.0. Explicitly whitelist allowed algorithms via the algorithms option in jwt.verify(). Rotate all secrets and invalidate existing sessions after upgrade.

question: Q1,Q3
title: express-jwt 0.1.3 - Pre-Security-Fix Version with No Algorithm Validation (Current: 8.x)
exact_code: "express-jwt": "0.1.3"
data_flow: express-jwt middleware validates JWT on incoming HTTP requests → passes decoded payload as req.user → downstream route handlers trust req.user for authorization decisions
exploit_scenario: express-jwt 0.1.3 is approximately 17 major versions behind current 8.x. This version does not enforce algorithm checking and does not handle algorithm confusion, potentially passing malformed or unsigned tokens through as valid. Combined with jsonwebtoken 0.4.0, an attacker can craft a JWT with alg:none and arbitrary claims including admin role, then gain full admin access without valid credentials. The devDependency @types/express-jwt references ^6.0.4 — a different version than what is installed — confirming known version mismatch.
framework_protection: None — algorithm validation was added in express-jwt 5.x.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to express-jwt ^8.0.0. Set the algorithms option explicitly in middleware configuration. Audit all routes using req.user for authorization bypass vulnerabilities.

question: Q2
title: MD5 Used for Password Hashing — No Salt, Cryptographically Broken
exact_code: export const hash = (data: string) => crypto.createHash('md5').update(data).digest('hex')
data_flow: User plaintext password → security.hash() → MD5 hex digest → stored in Users.password column (models/user.ts line 281). Same hash used in login SQL query (routes/login.ts line 384) and 2FA password verification (routes/2fa.ts lines 549, 594).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker obtains the SQLite/database dump. 2. All password hashes are unsalted MD5. 3. Attacker runs a GPU cracker (hashcat -m 0) against a rainbow table or wordlist. Typical MD5 wordlist crack: seconds to minutes. 4. Admin password 'admin123' → MD5 0192023a7bbd73250516f069df18b500 is in every public rainbow table. 5. Full account takeover of all users.
framework_protection: Node.js crypto module does not prevent weak algorithm selection. No Sequelize-level password hashing hook using bcrypt is in place.
fix_recommendation: Replace with bcrypt (cost ≥12), scrypt, or argon2id. Update the User model setter: `const bcrypt = require('bcrypt'); this.setDataValue('password', bcrypt.hashSync(clearTextPassword, 12))`. Update all comparison sites to use `bcrypt.compareSync()`.

question: Q5
title: RSA Private Key Hardcoded Inline in Source Code
exact_code: const privateKey = '-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----\r\\r\n-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----'
data_flow: privateKey literal in insecurity.ts → used in jwt.sign() (line 56) for all JWT issuance → used in crypto.createHmac() for deluxeToken generation (line 152). The same key signs every user session token.
exploit_scenario: 1. Any developer with repo access (or anyone who reads the public GitHub repo) has the private key. 2. Attacker forges a JWT with role='admin', signs it with the extracted private key. 3. Attacker sends the forged token to any authenticated endpoint → full admin access. 4. The key also allows minting unlimited deluxeTokens for any email address.
framework_protection: jsonwebtoken does not validate that signing keys are externally managed. The key is committed to git history permanently.
fix_recommendation: Load the private key from an environment variable or secrets manager at runtime: `const privateKey = process.env.JWT_PRIVATE_KEY`. Never commit private keys to source control. Rotate the key immediately. Add .gitignore entries for key files.

question: Q6
title: Encryption Keys Served via Unauthenticated Public HTTP Endpoint
data_flow: HTTP GET /encryptionkeys/:file → no authentication check → reads file from 'encryptionkeys/' directory → sends file contents to unauthenticated caller. The JWT public key (jwt.pub) is served here, and the directory may contain other key material.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker sends GET /encryptionkeys/jwt.pub → receives RSA public key. 2. Since the private key is hardcoded in insecurity.ts, attacker already has it. 3. The key server makes both halves of the RSA keypair publicly accessible via HTTP, confirming the JWT signing keys. 4. For any algorithm=none or RS→HS confusion attacks, having the public key enables forging HS256 tokens signed with the public key bytes.
framework_protection: No authentication middleware is applied to the /encryptionkeys route. The directory traversal is partially mitigated (no '/' in filename) but path traversal via URL encoding is not explicitly blocked.
fix_recommendation: Remove the /encryptionkeys public route entirely. If public key distribution is needed for verification purposes, serve only the public key from a documented, rate-limited endpoint. Never serve private keys over HTTP under any circumstances.

question: Q1
title: RSA private key hardcoded in source file (insecurity.ts)
exact_code: const privateKey = '-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----\r\nMIICXAIBAAKBgQDNwqLEe9wgTXCbC7+RPdDbBbeqjdbs4kOPOIGzqLpXvJXlxxW8iMz0EaM4BKUqYsIa+ndv3NAn2RxCd5ubVdJJcX43zO6Ko0TFEZx...'
data_flow: The RSA private key used for JWT signing is a hardcoded string literal in source code. Any log line, stack trace, or module dump that serialises this module exposes the key. The key is also committed to version control, accessible to every developer and CI/CD system.
exploit_scenario: Attacker clones the public repo (or obtains a log dump), extracts the private key, and mints arbitrary JWT tokens for any user including admin. Full authentication bypass with no brute-force required.
framework_protection: No framework protection exists. The private key is never read from an environment variable or secrets manager.
fix_recommendation: Load the private key from an environment variable or a secrets manager (e.g. AWS Secrets Manager, Vault). Never commit cryptographic material to source control. Rotate the exposed key immediately.

question: Q1
title: SQL Injection via product search query parameter
exact_code: models.sequelize.query(`SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ((name LIKE '%${criteria}%' OR description LIKE '%${criteria}%') AND deletedAt IS NULL) ORDER BY name`)
data_flow: req.query.q (GET /rest/products/search) → criteria variable (truncated to 200 chars, no sanitization) → raw Sequelize query string via template literal
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends GET /rest/products/search?q=')) UNION SELECT id,email,password,role,username,NULL,NULL,NULL,NULL FROM Users-- to exfiltrate all user credentials. The 200-char limit does not prevent UNION-based extraction. No authentication required.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Use parameterized query: models.sequelize.query('SELECT * FROM Products WHERE (name LIKE :criteria OR description LIKE :criteria) AND deletedAt IS NULL ORDER BY name', { replacements: { criteria: "%"+criteria+"%" } })

question: Q1
title: SQL Injection in login endpoint via email field
exact_code: models.sequelize.query(`SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = '${req.body.email || ''}' AND password = '${security.hash(req.body.password || '')}' AND deletedAt IS NULL`, { model: UserModel, plain: true })
data_flow: req.body.email (POST /rest/user/login) → template literal interpolation → raw SQL executed by Sequelize. Password is MD5-hashed but email has no sanitization.
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends POST /rest/user/login with email value of: ' OR 1=1-- to bypass authentication and log in as the first user (admin). Classic SQLi auth bypass. No prior authentication needed.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Replace with ORM call: UserModel.findOne({ where: { email: req.body.email, password: security.hash(req.body.password), deletedAt: null } })

question: Q3
title: NoSQL injection via $where JavaScript expression in trackOrder
exact_code: db.ordersCollection.find({ $where: `this.orderId === '${id}'` })
data_flow: req.params.id (GET /rest/track-order/:id) → when reflectedXssChallenge enabled, id is only truncated to 60 chars (no sanitization) → concatenated into MongoDB $where JS expression
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends GET /rest/track-order/x%27%20||%20true%20||%20%27 — the $where clause becomes: this.orderId === 'x' || true || '' which evaluates to true for every document, returning all orders in the collection.
framework_protection: partial
fix_recommendation: Replace $where with a direct field query: db.ordersCollection.find({ orderId: id }) after validating id against a strict alphanumeric/hyphen pattern. Never pass user input into $where.

question: Q3
title: NoSQL injection via $where and event-loop blocking sleep in showProductReviews
exact_code: db.reviewsCollection.find({ $where: 'this.product == ' + id })
data_flow: req.params.id (GET /rest/products/:id/reviews) → when noSqlCommandChallenge is enabled, left as string truncated to 40 chars → concatenated into $where JS string
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends id = '1 || sleep(2000)' to trigger the globally-registered sleep() function (which blocks the Node.js event loop for up to 2 seconds). Also: id = '1; return true' returns all reviews across all products. The global.sleep on lines 747-755 is specifically wired for this exploit.
framework_protection: partial
fix_recommendation: Use direct field query: db.reviewsCollection.find({ product: Number(id) }) with strict numeric validation. Remove the global.sleep registration — it is a deliberate DoS primitive.

question: Q4
title: Remote code execution via vm.runInContext evaluating user-controlled orderLinesData
data_flow: req.body.orderLinesData (POST /b2b/v2/orders, JWT auth required) → placed into vm sandbox → evaluated by notevil safeEval. vm.createContext does NOT prevent prototype-chain escapes.
exploit_scenario: Attacker with valid JWT sends a notevil sandbox-escape payload as orderLinesData. Known escapes include accessing constructor.constructor('return process')() to get the host process object, achieving full RCE. Even without a full escape, sending an infinite loop causes a 503 DoS (the rceOccupyChallenge confirms this is intentional).
framework_protection: bypassed
fix_recommendation: Remove eval entirely. Accept structured JSON for order line items and validate against a schema. If expression evaluation is required, run it in an isolated subprocess with no access to Node globals.

question: Q6
title: Sequelize raw query with template literal interpolation in searchProducts (ORM bypass)
exact_code: models.sequelize.query(`SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ((name LIKE '%${criteria}%' OR description LIKE '%${criteria}%') AND deletedAt IS NULL) ORDER BY name`)
data_flow: req.query.q → criteria → models.sequelize.query() called with raw string and no replacements/bind object — Sequelize ORM parameterization completely bypassed
exploit_scenario: Identical to INJ-001. Noted separately as the canonical Q6 finding: Sequelize's .query() API supports safe parameterization via replacements or bind but is called here with a raw template literal, providing zero protection.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: models.sequelize.query('SELECT * FROM Products WHERE (name LIKE :q OR description LIKE :q) AND deletedAt IS NULL ORDER BY name', { replacements: { q: "%"+criteria+"%" }, type: QueryTypes.SELECT })

question: Q6
title: Sequelize raw query with template literal interpolation in login (ORM bypass)
exact_code: models.sequelize.query(`SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = '${req.body.email || ''}' AND password = '${security.hash(req.body.password || '')}' AND deletedAt IS NULL`, { model: UserModel, plain: true })
data_flow: req.body.email → template literal → models.sequelize.query() with no replacements/bind — the { model, plain } options only control result formatting, not query safety
exploit_scenario: Identical to INJ-002. Noted separately as Q6 finding: passing { model: UserModel } to a raw query does not add parameterization — Sequelize still executes the raw string as-is.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: UserModel.findOne({ where: { email: req.body.email, password: security.hash(req.body.password ?? ''), deletedAt: null } })

question: Q8
title: In-memory AdmissionReceiptChain is lost on restart — audit trail integrity breaks across restarts
finding: _receipt_chain = AdmissionReceiptChain() is initialized at module load time as a plain Python object with a list stored in self._receipts. This is never persisted to disk, a database, or any external store. On service restart: (1) the entire receipt chain is lost; (2) the next receipt created will have prev_receipt_hash=None (genesis), creating a fresh chain with no link to pre-restart receipts; (3) the replay endpoint (/v1/memory/replay) searches only _receipt_chain.receipts — any receipt from before a restart will return RECEIPT_NOT_FOUND and is_legal=False; (4) there is no mechanism to reload or reconstruct the chain from the SQLite store (the v1_memories table stores receipt_hash per memory, but the chain structure and the full receipt objects are not persisted). The code comment at line 49 acknowledges this: '# Global receipt chain (in production, use persistent store)'.
exact_lines:
# Global receipt chain (in production, use persistent store)
_receipt_chain = AdmissionReceiptChain()

# AdmissionReceiptChain.__init__:
def __init__(self, genesis_receipt: Optional[AdmissionReceipt] = None):
    self._receipts: List[AdmissionReceipt] = []
    self._head_hash: Optional[str] = None

# Replay endpoint searches only in-memory chain:
for i, receipt in enumerate(_receipt_chain.receipts):
    if receipt.receipt_hash == request.receipt_hash:
        receipt_found = receipt
        chain_position = i
        break
line_approx: ~line 49 of routes/memory_v1.py; ~lines 2684-2698 of admission_receipts.py; ~lines 652-656 of routes/memory_v1.py
exploit_scenario: Any restart (crash, deployment, OOM kill) silently resets the audit chain. An attacker who induces a service restart after writing malicious content has effectively erased the admission receipt for that content — replay will return is_legal=False (RECEIPT_NOT_FOUND), making the content appear unattested. Simultaneously, all legitimate pre-restart receipts become unverifiable, breaking forensic audit capability entirely.

question: Q2
title: Admin account seeded with hardcoded password 'admin123' on every startup
exact_code:
- email: admin
  password: 'admin123'
  key: admin
  role: 'admin'
data_flow: server.ts calls start() → datacreator() → createUsers() → loads users.yml → UserModel.create({ email: 'admin@<domain>', password: 'admin123', role: 'admin' }). The admin account is recreated from scratch on every sequelize.sync({ force: true }) (line 724 server.ts), meaning the password is reset to 'admin123' on every server restart. The login route (routes/login.ts line 60) explicitly verifies this credential to mark the weakPasswordChallenge as solved, confirming the credential is functional.
exploit_scenario: 1. Application domain defaults to juice-sh.op (config). 2. Attacker posts to /rest/user/login with { email: 'admin@juice-sh.op', password: 'admin123' }. 3. Server returns a signed JWT with role:'admin'. 4. Attacker has full administrative access: can view all user data, orders, feedback, and access /administration panel.
framework_protection: Default/seed credentials for privileged accounts must be randomised at first-start and stored in a secrets manager, or forced-change on first login. The application must never ship with a known static admin password.
fix_recommendation: Replace the static password in users.yml with a crypto.randomBytes(32) value generated at seed time and written to a secure secret store (env var / vault). Add a 'mustChangePassword' flag and enforce rotation on first login for all admin accounts.

question: Q2
title: RSA private key hardcoded in source code (lib/insecurity.ts)
exact_code: const privateKey = '-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----\r\nMIICXAIBAAKBgQDNwqLEe9wgTXCbC7+RPdDbBbeqjdbs4kOPOIGzqLpXvJXlxxW8iMz0EaM4BKUqYsIa+ndv3NAn2RxCd5ubVdJJcX43zO6Ko0TFEZx/65gY3BE0O6syCEmUP4qbSd6exou/F+WTISzbQ5FBVPVmhnYhG/kpwt/cIxK5iUn5hm+4tQIDAQABAoGBAI+8xiPoOrA+KMnG/T4jJsG6TsHQcDHvJi7o1IKC/hnIXha0...(truncated)\r\n-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----'
data_flow: The hardcoded privateKey is used by: (1) jwt.sign(user, privateKey, { expiresIn: '6h', algorithm: 'RS256' }) — minting all authentication JWTs; (2) crypto.createHmac('sha256', privateKey) in deluxeToken() — generating deluxe membership tokens. Anyone with access to the repository (public GitHub) can extract this key and forge arbitrary JWTs for any user including admin.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker reads the public repository and extracts the RSA private key from lib/insecurity.ts. 2. Attacker crafts a JWT payload: { data: { id: 1, email: 'admin@juice-sh.op', role: 'admin' } } signed with the known private key using RS256. 3. Attacker sends this token in the Authorization header. 4. expressJwt verifies it against the known public key (also file-readable) and grants admin access without needing a password.
framework_protection: Private keys must never be committed to source control. They should be loaded from environment variables or a secrets manager at runtime. The encryptionkeys/jwt.pub public key is correctly read from disk; the private key should follow the same pattern.
fix_recommendation: Remove the hardcoded key. Load it via: const privateKey = process.env.JWT_PRIVATE_KEY ?? fs.readFileSync('encryptionkeys/jwt.key', 'utf8'). Rotate the key immediately since the current key is already compromised by its presence in version control.

question: Q3
title: RCE via vm.runInContext evaluating user-supplied orderLinesData
exact_code:
const orderLinesData = body.orderLinesData || ''
const sandbox = { safeEval, orderLinesData }
vm.createContext(sandbox)
vm.runInContext('safeEval(orderLinesData)', sandbox, { timeout: 2000 })
data_flow: HTTP POST body.orderLinesData -> vm sandbox context -> vm.runInContext evaluates safeEval(orderLinesData). The user-controlled string is the expression being evaluated inside the notevil wrapper.
exploit_scenario: Attacker POSTs a crafted payload with orderLinesData containing a notevil sandbox escape via constructor chain. Node.js vm module explicitly documents it is NOT a security boundary. Known vm escapes via __proto__ or constructor.constructor yield arbitrary code execution in the server process.
framework_protection: Partial: notevil wraps eval to restrict dangerous operations; vm.createContext provides some isolation. However, the Node.js vm module is not a security sandbox — escapes are well-documented. The 2000ms timeout only limits DoS, not code execution.
fix_recommendation: Remove user-controlled eval entirely. If expression evaluation is required, parse and evaluate only a strict whitelist of allowed arithmetic/logic operations using a purpose-built AST evaluator. Never pass raw user input to vm.runInContext or any eval-like function.

title: S3MaintenanceHalt NOT caught by maintenance route except S3MaintenanceHalt — but caught by summarize/prune fallback `except Exception: payload = {}`
finding:
In maintenance_summarize() and maintenance_prune(), the JSON parsing block uses `except Exception: payload = {}`. Since S3MaintenanceHalt inherits from BaseException (not Exception), this specific handler does NOT catch it. However, the authority extraction call `_extract_maintenance_authority(payload)` is INSIDE the try block that follows — and S3MaintenanceHalt IS caught by the explicit `except S3MaintenanceHalt` handler at the bottom of each route handler. This is correct.

However, the real issue is with `maintenance_summarize` and `maintenance_prune` where the JSON parse failure results in `payload = {}` (empty dict). With an empty payload, `_extract_maintenance_authority({})` will raise an HTTPException(403) for missing authority_type — which is correct. But the prune route has a subtle difference: if JSON parsing raises an exception in the prune route's `try/except Exception: payload = {}` block, then `older_than_days = payload.get('older_than_days', 90)` returns 90 — the DEFAULT retention floor. A client that sends malformed JSON to the prune endpoint will get the prune operation attempted with the default 90-day floor. This is not bypassed because authority is still required, but the behavior is surprising.

The true critical issue: In maintenance_tombstone(), if S3MaintenanceHalt is raised BEFORE the `try` block (during `_extract_maintenance_authority`), it propagates correctly. But the `_handle_maintenance_result()` function is defined but NEVER CALLED in any of the three maintenance routes. Instead, each route has duplicated, inconsistent result handling logic. In `maintenance_summarize` and `maintenance_tombstone`, the routes call `svc.summarize()` / `svc.tombstone()` which internally call `admit_maintenance_operation()`. But the routes then check `result.get('admitted', True)` — note the DEFAULT IS TRUE. This means if the result dict has no 'admitted' key (e.g., an unexpected dict shape from a future code change), the check evaluates to `not True = False`, meaning the route RETURNS SUCCESS instead of raising 403.
exploit_scenario: If MaintenanceService.summarize() or tombstone() returns a dict that lacks the 'admitted' key (e.g., due to a bug or future refactor), `result.get('admitted', True)` evaluates to True, and the route returns HTTP 200 with the result dict — even if the operation was not actually admitted. This is a fail-OPEN default on the admission check gate in the route handler.

title: AuditSinkRegistry is an in-memory singleton — audit records are lost on process restart (LC-8 equivalent for v2)
finding: AuditSinkRegistry uses a module-level singleton pattern (cls._instance). All AppendOnlyAuditSink instances are stored in memory (_sinks dict). There is no persistence layer. On any process restart, crash, or container restart, all audit entries are lost. The seal_and_archive() method returns the JSON trace but does not write it anywhere (no file, no DB, no queue). There is no persistent audit store. This is the exact same class of issue as LC-8 (receipt chain ephemeral) from v1, now applying to the audit sink. The hash-chaining, ordering guarantees, and tamper-detection all become meaningless if the chain is not durable.
exploit_scenario: The service process restarts (normal deployment, crash, OOM kill). All audit entries from active runs are permanently lost. An attacker who can trigger a controlled restart (e.g., via OOM or crafted request that causes a 500) can erase the audit trail for any operations that occurred in that process lifetime.

title: Receipt chain in v2 is still an in-memory singleton — same as v1 LC-8
finding: The AuditSinkRegistry (the v2 equivalent of the v1 receipt chain) is an in-memory singleton with no persistence (see V2-12 for full detail). This is structurally identical to the v1 LC-8 finding. The v2 design adds hash-chaining and thread-safety improvements, but the fundamental durability gap remains: all audit records are lost on process restart. The fact that the WRITE_ADMITTED events are never even emitted (V2-19) means this finding is compounded — there is no write receipt in the first place, and even if there were, it would not survive a restart.
exact_lines: cls._instance._sinks: Dict[str, AppendOnlyAuditSink] = {}
exploit_scenario: Same as LC-8: any process restart erases the audit trail. An attacker who can trigger a controlled restart (OOM, crafted 500-producing request) can erase all in-flight audit records.

question: Q2
title: IDOR: Basket Retrieval Returns Any User's Basket Without Enforcing Ownership
data_flow: req.params.id (attacker-controlled) → BasketModel.findOne({ where: { id } }) → res.json(basket). No WHERE clause scoping result to the authenticated user's bid. The challengeUtils.solveIf call only records the challenge as solved — it does NOT block or reject the response.
exploit_scenario: Attacker authenticates as user A (basket ID 2), then sends GET /rest/basket/1. The server fetches basket 1 (belonging to user B) and returns its full contents — products, quantities, prices — to the attacker. No 401/403 is issued; only a challenge flag is set server-side.
framework_protection: Route is protected by security.isAuthorized() (JWT required) and security.appendUserId() for the /rest/basket prefix (server.ts line 1728), but neither middleware scopes the basket lookup to the authenticated user's own basket ID.
fix_recommendation: After fetching the basket, compare basket.UserId to the authenticated user's ID and return 403 if they differ: `if (basket.UserId !== user.data.id) { res.status(403).json({ error: 'Access denied' }); return; }`

question: Q1
title: Basket Item Addition Bypasses Ownership Check via Duplicate JSON Key Parsing
data_flow: utils.parseJsonCustom parses raw body into key-value pairs, allowing duplicate keys. Ownership check uses basketIds[0] (first occurrence) but the saved BasketId is basketIds[basketIds.length-1] (last occurrence). An attacker sends JSON with BasketId appearing twice: first with their own ID (passes the check), then with victim's ID (used for insert).
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends POST /api/BasketItems with body: {"ProductId":1,"BasketId":2,"BasketId":5,"quantity":1} where 2 is attacker's basket and 5 is victim's basket. The check passes (basketIds[0]=2 matches user.bid=2) but the item is inserted into basket 5.
framework_protection: Route has security.isAuthorized() and security.appendUserId() applied. The ownership check exists but only validates the first key when a custom non-standard JSON parser is used, creating a split-check/split-use flaw.
fix_recommendation: Reject requests where basketIds contains more than one distinct value. Also enforce: verify that the resolved BasketId (last value) matches the authenticated user's bid before saving.

question: Q5
title: Wildcard CORS Policy Allows Any Origin to Make Credentialed Cross-Origin Requests
data_flow: cors() called with no configuration defaults to Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *. Every API response carries this header, permitting any web origin to read the response body of authenticated endpoints.
exploit_scenario: A malicious site at https://evil.com hosts JavaScript that calls fetch('https://juiceshop.example.com/api/Users', {credentials:'include'}). The wildcard CORS policy means the browser delivers the response to the attacker's page, leaking all user records. The comment 'Allow everything!' confirms this is intentional but constitutes a real vulnerability.
framework_protection: None. The comment explicitly names this a 'Bludgeon solution'. No origin allowlist, no vary header, no credentials restriction.
fix_recommendation: Replace bare cors() with a configured allowlist: cors({ origin: ['https://yourdomain.com'], credentials: true }). Never combine credentials:true with a wildcard origin.

question: Q3
title: Admin Application-Version and Application-Configuration Endpoints Lack Any Authorization
exact_code:
app.get('/rest/admin/application-version', retrieveAppVersion())
app.get('/rest/admin/application-configuration', retrieveAppConfiguration())
data_flow: Requests to /rest/admin/application-version and /rest/admin/application-configuration pass through no isAuthorized(), isAdmin(), or role-check middleware before reaching the handler. Any unauthenticated HTTP client can retrieve these endpoints.
exploit_scenario: Unauthenticated attacker runs: curl http://localhost:3000/rest/admin/application-configuration and receives the full application configuration including internal paths, feature flags, and potentially sensitive settings that aid further attacks.
framework_protection: Other admin-tier routes (e.g., /rest/order-history/orders, /rest/order-history/:id/delivery-status) do use security.isAccounting(). These two endpoints have no equivalent guard despite carrying an /admin/ path prefix.
fix_recommendation: Add security.isAuthorized() and an admin role check middleware before both handlers: app.get('/rest/admin/application-version', security.isAuthorized(), isAdmin(), retrieveAppVersion()). Define isAdmin() analogously to isAccounting() checking role === roles.admin.

question: Q4
title: Log Files Served Without Authentication; Path Traversal via Null Byte Not Fully Mitigated
data_flow: GET /support/logs/:file → params.file → path.resolve('logs/', file) → res.sendFile(). Server.ts line 1656 registers app.use('/support/logs/:file', serveLogFiles()) with no preceding auth middleware. The check blocks '/' but does not block '%2F' (URL-decoded by Express before the check), and does not block '..' sequences.
exploit_scenario: Unauthenticated attacker requests GET /support/logs/access.log.2026-04-10 and receives the full HTTP access log revealing IP addresses, user agents, request paths, and timing of all users. Access logs may contain sensitive URL query parameters (e.g., search terms, tokens in URLs).
framework_protection: Directory listing at /support/logs is also enabled via serveIndex (server.ts line 1654) with no auth guard, making log file names discoverable. verify.accessControlChallenges() is applied but only records a challenge flag — it does not block access.
fix_recommendation: Add security.isAuthorized() and an admin/accounting role check before serveLogFiles(). Remove public directory listing from /support/logs.

question: Q3
title: User Registration Accepts Arbitrary 'role' Field Allowing Self-Promotion to Admin
data_flow: POST /api/Users → verify.registerAdminChallenge() → next() → finale autoModel handler creates User record with all body fields including role. The challenge middleware only records that role=admin was submitted — it does not strip or reject the field. finale-rest passes req.body directly to Sequelize create(), persisting the attacker-supplied role value.
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends POST /api/Users with body {"email":"attacker@evil.com","password":"pass","passwordRepeat":"pass","role":"admin"}. The server creates an admin account. The attacker logs in and gains full administrative access to the application.
framework_protection: verify.registerAdminChallenge() is applied (server.ts line 1792) but only as a challenge tracker. No middleware strips the role field from req.body before the ORM layer persists it.
fix_recommendation: Add middleware before finale takes over to explicitly delete req.body.role and req.body.isAdmin before the user record is created: `app.post('/api/Users', (req, res, next) => { delete req.body.role; next() })`. Alternatively, configure finale to use an explicit allowlist of accepted fields.

question: Q3
title: JWT 'none' Algorithm Not Explicitly Blocked; jws.verify Signature Weakness
exact_code:
export const verify = (token: string) => token ? (jws.verify as ((token: string, secret: string) => boolean))(token, publicKey) : false
export const decode = (token: string) => { return jws.decode(token)?.payload }
data_flow: verify() uses the jws library to validate the JWT signature. jws.verify() behavior with algorithm 'none' depends on library version. decode() is called after verify() in 2fa.ts: `security.verify(tmpToken) && security.decode(tmpToken)` — the decoded payload is used for authorization without re-checking algorithm type.
exploit_scenario: Older versions of jws (and jsonwebtoken < 9.0) accept tokens with alg:'none' as valid. An attacker can forge a JWT with alg:none and an arbitrary payload (e.g., role:'admin') and send it to protected endpoints. Even if jws blocks it, the code flow decodes the payload separately without verifying the algorithm field, creating a potential SSRF/confusion path.
framework_protection: partial
fix_recommendation: Use jsonwebtoken's jwt.verify() with an explicit algorithms allowlist: jwt.verify(token, publicKey, { algorithms: ['RS256'] }). Never use jws.verify() which has a weaker API contract. Explicitly reject tokens where decoded header.alg !== 'RS256'.

question: Q4
title: Session Tokens Stored In-Memory Only (No Persistence, Single-Instance State)
data_flow: All authenticated sessions are stored in a plain in-memory JavaScript object (tokenMap/idMap) on the server process. There is no TTL or expiry on the in-memory session store. JWT tokens themselves expire in 6h (authorize: expiresIn: '6h'), but revocation is impossible since the tokenMap has no cleanup mechanism.
exploit_scenario: 1) A stolen JWT token that has not expired cannot be invalidated — logout does not remove it from tokenMap or blacklist it. 2) Server restart clears all sessions without warning. 3) In a clustered deployment, sessions are not shared between nodes, causing unpredictable auth failures.
framework_protection: partial
fix_recommendation: Implement a proper session store (Redis or DB-backed) with TTL matching JWT expiry. Implement token revocation on logout by maintaining a blacklist or using short-lived tokens + refresh token rotation. Add periodic cleanup of expired entries from tokenMap.

question: Q4
title: Cookie Token Missing httpOnly and Secure Flags
exact_code: res.cookie('token', token)
data_flow: In updateAuthenticatedUsers() middleware, the JWT is set as a cookie via res.cookie('token', token) with no options object. No httpOnly, no Secure, no SameSite flags are specified. The cookieParser is initialized with secret 'kekse' (server.ts line 1293) but the auth cookie itself is not signed.
exploit_scenario: Without httpOnly, client-side JavaScript (e.g., via XSS) can read document.cookie to steal the auth token. Without Secure, the token can be transmitted over HTTP and intercepted via network sniffing. Without SameSite, the cookie is vulnerable to CSRF-based session hijacking.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Set cookie options: res.cookie('token', token, { httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'strict', maxAge: 6 * 60 * 60 * 1000 }). Also configure the application to enforce HTTPS.

question: Q4
title: Change Password Endpoint Accepts Parameters via URL Query String
data_flow: The change-password route is registered as a GET request, and all password parameters (current, new, repeat) are passed as URL query parameters. GET /rest/user/change-password?current=X&new=Y&repeat=Y. Passwords appear in server access logs, browser history, referrer headers, and proxy logs.
exploit_scenario: 1) Passwords in URLs are logged by morgan (combined format) in access.log files, which are publicly readable via /support/logs directory listing. 2) Referer header leaks new password to any third-party resource loaded on the page. 3) Browser history and shared bookmarks expose the new password. The access log disclosure is particularly severe — an attacker who reads /support/logs sees every password change in plaintext.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Convert to POST with password parameters in the request body (application/json). Remove the GET handler. Ensure morgan logging excludes or redacts the request body for sensitive endpoints.

question: Q5
title: Password Reset via Security Questions — Guessable Answers, No Rate Limiting
data_flow: POST /rest/user/reset-password accepts email + security answer + new password. It looks up the user's stored security answer (HMAC-SHA256), compares against submitted answer, and resets the password on match. Rate limiting is applied (100 req per 5 min window) but uses the X-Forwarded-For header as the key generator, which is attacker-controlled.
exploit_scenario: 1) Security question answers are highly guessable (mother's maiden name, pet name, etc.) — social engineering or OSINT trivially reveals them. 2) The rate limit key is extracted from X-Forwarded-For header: `headers['X-Forwarded-For'] ?? ip` (server.ts line 1350). An attacker can rotate this header to bypass the 100-request limit entirely, enabling brute force of security answers. 3) No email verification or secondary confirmation is required — password reset is instant on correct answer.
framework_protection: bypassed
fix_recommendation: 1) Replace security questions with email-based one-time reset links (time-limited, single-use tokens stored in DB). 2) Fix rate limit key to use req.ip only (remove X-Forwarded-For trust or sanitize it). 3) If security questions are retained, implement exponential backoff and account lockout after N failed attempts.

question: Q6
title: 2FA TOTP Secret Stored in Plaintext in Database
data_flow: The TOTP secret is stored as a plaintext STRING in the Users.totpSecret column. It is set via userModel.totpSecret = secret in routes/2fa.ts setup(). The challenge name 'twoFactorAuthUnsafeSecretStorageChallenge' in 2fa.ts verify() confirms this is a known intentional vulnerability.
exploit_scenario: An attacker who gains read access to the Users table (e.g., via SQL injection) obtains every user's TOTP secret in plaintext. Combined with cracked MD5 passwords, this completely defeats 2FA — the attacker has both factors without any additional effort.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Encrypt TOTP secrets at rest using AES-256-GCM with a key loaded from a secrets manager (not hardcoded). Decrypt only in memory at verification time. Never log or expose totpSecret in API responses.

question: Q1,Q3
title: sanitize-html 1.4.2 - XSS Bypass via Outdated HTML Parser (Current: 2.x)
exact_code: "sanitize-html": "1.4.2"
data_flow: User-supplied HTML input → sanitize-html 1.4.2 → stored in database or rendered to page → reflected back to other users in product reviews and feedback forms
exploit_scenario: sanitize-html 1.4.2 (released circa 2016) has documented XSS bypass vectors fixed in subsequent versions. CVE-2021-26540 (1.x series) allows bypass via crafted tag attributes. Multiple parser confusion issues allow javascript: URIs in href and src attributes to survive sanitization. Attacker posts a review containing crafted HTML with a malicious URI that the sanitizer fails to strip. When another user views the review, the XSS payload executes in their browser, stealing session tokens or performing actions on their behalf.
framework_protection: Angular's template engine provides some protection for Angular-rendered content, but sanitize-html is used server-side for content stored in DB and served via API. Server-side sanitization failures bypass Angular's client-side DomSanitizer.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to sanitize-html ^2.13.0. Review and tighten allowedTags and allowedAttributes allowlists. Test with known bypass payloads from OWASP XSS cheat sheet after upgrade.

question: Q1,Q3
title: libxmljs2 ~0.37.0 - XXE Vulnerability via Default External Entity Processing
data_flow: User uploads XML file or provides XML input → libxmljs2 parses XML with default settings → external entity expansion enabled by default → file system read or SSRF possible via XXE
exploit_scenario: libxmljs2 uses libxml2 under the hood with external entity processing enabled by default. An attacker submits XML containing a DOCTYPE with a SYSTEM entity referencing local files (file:///etc/passwd) or internal HTTP endpoints. The parser resolves the entity, reading local files or making server-side HTTP requests. SSRF can be escalated to access cloud metadata endpoints to steal IAM credentials. The tilde range ~0.37.0 locks to patch versions of 0.37.x only, blocking automatic security patches from 0.38+.
framework_protection: None in libxmljs2 0.37.x — external entities must be explicitly disabled by passing options to parseXml().
fix_recommendation: Pass { noent: false, dtdload: false, dtdvalid: false } to all libxmljs2.parseXml() calls. Upgrade to the latest libxmljs2 version. Consider replacing with fast-xml-parser with processEntities disabled.

question: Q2,Q5
title: unzipper 0.9.15 - Outdated with Zip Slip (Path Traversal) History (Current: 0.12.x)
exact_code: "unzipper": "0.9.15"
data_flow: User uploads ZIP file → unzipper 0.9.15 extracts archive entries → entries written to filesystem → path traversal via crafted entry names can write outside the intended extraction directory
exploit_scenario: unzipper 0.9.15 is multiple minor versions behind current 0.12.x. Older unzipper versions are vulnerable to Zip Slip — path traversal via ../ sequences in ZIP entry filenames. An attacker uploads a ZIP archive containing an entry with a path-traversal filename targeting a sensitive system location. When extracted, the file is written outside the intended directory, potentially overwriting critical system or application files to enable unauthorized access or code execution. The fixed version 0.9.15 (not a caret range) means no automatic updates ever occur.
framework_protection: No path sanitization is applied to extracted filenames before writing in unzipper 0.9.x.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to unzipper ^0.12.0. Add explicit path traversal validation before writing each entry: verify that path.resolve(destDir, entry.path) starts with path.resolve(destDir). Consider adm-zip with explicit path validation.

question: Q3
title: js-yaml 3.14.0 - Dangerous load() Function Present, Fixed Only in 4.x
data_flow: js-yaml parses YAML configuration files or user-supplied YAML input → parsed objects used in application logic → the load() function in 3.x can execute arbitrary JavaScript when given untrusted YAML with JS-specific tags
exploit_scenario: js-yaml 3.x exposes yaml.load() which, unlike safeLoad(), permits YAML documents using JavaScript-specific tags that invoke arbitrary JavaScript at parse time. In js-yaml 4.x, load() was redesigned to be safe by default and safeLoad() was removed to eliminate the naming confusion. If any Juice Shop code uses js-yaml's load() on user-controlled input (such as uploaded YAML files in challenge scenarios), an attacker can supply YAML using JS function type tags to achieve remote code execution on the server. The ^3.14.0 range will never pull js-yaml 4.x where this vulnerability class is eliminated by design.
framework_protection: If all call sites use yaml.safeLoad() rather than yaml.load(), the risk is mitigated in 3.x — but this requires a code-level audit to confirm consistently across the codebase.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to js-yaml ^4.1.0 where load() is safe by default. Audit all require('js-yaml').load( call sites to confirm no untrusted input is passed. Search codebase for yaml.load( to identify risky usages.

question: Q3
title: pug 3.0.3 - Server-Side Template Injection Risk if User Input Reaches Template Position
data_flow: pug renders server-side HTML templates → if user input is concatenated into template strings rather than passed as template data variables → SSTI allows arbitrary server-side code execution
exploit_scenario: pug (formerly jade) is vulnerable to Server-Side Template Injection if user-controlled strings are passed directly to pug.compile() or pug.render() as the template source argument rather than as template data variables. If any Juice Shop route constructs a pug template by concatenating user input, an attacker can submit a payload using pug interpolation syntax that references Node.js globals to read sensitive files or execute operating system commands, achieving remote code execution. Even with correct data passing, older pug versions had unescaped interpolation leading to XSS in specific rendering contexts.
framework_protection: If user input is only passed as data (the second argument to pug.render(template, data)) and never as the template string itself, SSTI is not possible. This requires code-level verification of all render call sites.
fix_recommendation: Audit all pug.render(), pug.compile(), and res.render() calls to confirm user input is never in the template source position. Upgrade to the latest pug 3.x patch. Add strict input validation middleware for routes using dynamic template rendering.

question: Q4
title: errorhandler 1.5.1 in Production Dependencies - Dev-Only Middleware Exposing Stack Traces
exact_code: "errorhandler": "^1.5.1"
data_flow: errorhandler Express middleware intercepts unhandled errors → formats full stack traces including local variables → sends detailed internal error information in HTTP responses → production errors expose internal paths, source structure, and server configuration
exploit_scenario: errorhandler is explicitly designed for development use only — its own documentation warns against production use. When active in production, unhandled Express errors return full stack traces in HTTP responses, revealing: server file system paths, Node.js module structure, source code line snippets, database query strings if the error originated from the ORM, and internal network addresses. An attacker deliberately triggers error conditions (malformed input, invalid resource identifiers, boundary values) and reads the detailed stack trace to fingerprint the application stack, identify file paths, and discover attack vectors for further exploitation.
framework_protection: Express has a built-in production mode (NODE_ENV=production) that suppresses some error detail, but errorhandler overrides this suppression when registered as middleware.
fix_recommendation: Remove errorhandler from production dependencies or guard its registration with an environment check: register it only when NODE_ENV equals 'development'. Move to devDependencies. Replace with a production-safe error handler that logs internally and returns only a generic error message to clients.

question: Q1
title: Hardcoded HMAC Secret Key in Source Code
exact_code: export const hmac = (data: string) => crypto.createHmac('sha256', 'pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj').update(data).digest('hex')
data_flow: Hardcoded HMAC secret 'pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj' → used to compute HMAC-SHA256 of arbitrary data. Any code importing this function gets a deterministic, publicly-known MAC secret.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker reads the open-source repository. 2. Attacker knows the HMAC secret. 3. Wherever HMAC is used for integrity verification, the attacker can forge valid MACs for arbitrary payloads. 4. If used in coupon or order contexts, attacker mints valid discount tokens.
framework_protection: None. The secret is a string literal, fully visible in source.
fix_recommendation: Move the HMAC secret to an environment variable: `crypto.createHmac('sha256', process.env.HMAC_SECRET)`. Ensure the secret is at least 256 bits of random data generated with a CSPRNG.

question: Q4
title: Math.random() Used for Security-Sensitive Token Generation (denyAll secret)
exact_code: export const denyAll = () => expressJwt({ secret: '' + Math.random() } as any)
data_flow: Math.random() generates a floating-point secret → converted to string → used as JWT verification secret in expressJwt middleware. This is called to create a 'deny all' middleware, but the secret space is only 2^53 (IEEE 754 mantissa) and Math.random() is non-cryptographic (seeded by system time / process state).
exploit_scenario: 1. If an attacker can observe or influence Math.random() seeding (e.g., via timing side-channels or V8 PRNG knowledge), they can predict the secret. 2. A predicted secret allows forging JWTs that pass the denyAll middleware. 3. Even without prediction: the secret space of ~15 decimal digits is brute-forceable offline once a sample token is obtained.
framework_protection: Node.js Math.random() is explicitly documented as not cryptographically secure. No seeding control is provided.
fix_recommendation: Replace with `crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')` to generate a cryptographically random 256-bit secret. For denyAll semantics, also consider always rejecting by checking the token against a known-invalid value.

question: Q1
title: Hardcoded Ethereum Wallet Mnemonic in Source Code
exact_code:
const mnemonic = 'purpose betray marriage blame crunch monitor spin slide donate sport lift clutch'
const mnemonicWallet = HDNodeWallet.fromPhrase(mnemonic)
data_flow: Hardcoded BIP-39 mnemonic → HDNodeWallet.fromPhrase() → derives privateKey, publicKey, address → compared against req.body.privateKey for challenge validation. The mnemonic is the root secret from which all wallet keys derive.
exploit_scenario: 1. Anyone reading the source (public GitHub) has the mnemonic. 2. Attacker calls HDNodeWallet.fromPhrase('purpose betray marriage blame crunch monitor spin slide donate sport lift clutch') to derive the private key. 3. Attacker immediately solves the nftUnlockChallenge. 4. If any real funds were sent to the derived address (0x* from this mnemonic), they are immediately stolen.
framework_protection: ethers.js HDNodeWallet does not validate that mnemonics are secret.
fix_recommendation: For a CTF/challenge context this is intentional. For any production system, mnemonics must never be hardcoded. Load from environment: `process.env.WALLET_MNEMONIC`. Rotate immediately if exposed.

question: Q1
title: Multiple Plaintext Passwords Hardcoded in Login Challenge Verification Logic
exact_code:
challengeUtils.solveIf(challenges.loginSupportChallenge, () => { return req.body.email === 'support@' + config.get<string>('application.domain') && req.body.password === 'J6aVjTgOpRs@?5l!Zkq2AYnCE@RF$P' })
challengeUtils.solveIf(challenges.loginRapperChallenge, () => { return req.body.email === 'mc.safesearch@' + config.get<string>('application.domain') && req.body.password === 'Mr. N00dles' })
challengeUtils.solveIf(challenges.loginAmyChallenge, () => { return req.body.email === 'amy@' + config.get<string>('application.domain') && req.body.password === 'K1f.....................' })
challengeUtils.solveIf(challenges.dlpPasswordSprayingChallenge, () => { return req.body.email === 'J12934@' + config.get<string>('application.domain') && req.body.password === '0Y8rMnww$*9VFYE§59-!Fg1L6t&6lB' })
challengeUtils.solveIf(challenges.oauthUserPasswordChallenge, () => { return req.body.email === 'bjoern.kimminich@gmail.com' && req.body.password === 'bW9jLmxpYW1nQGhjaW5pbW1pay5ucmVvamI=' })
data_flow: Hardcoded plaintext passwords in verifyPreLoginChallenges() → compared directly against req.body.password during every login attempt → these are the real account passwords for seeded users, stored in source code.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker reads source code. 2. Attacker logs in directly using email/password pairs: support@[domain] / J6aVjTgOpRs@?5l!Zkq2AYnCE@RF$P, etc. 3. Immediate account takeover of support, accounting, and admin-adjacent accounts. 4. The base64-encoded password for bjoern.kimminich@gmail.com decodes to 'bjoern.kimminich@gmail.com' reversed — an additional weak password pattern.
framework_protection: None. These are plaintext string comparisons in server-side code.
fix_recommendation: Never hardcode credentials in source. For challenge detection, compare against hashed values or use a separate challenge-flag mechanism that does not require knowing the plaintext password at comparison time.

question: Q1
title: Security answer logged in plaintext during creation
exact_code: logger.error(`Could not insert SecurityAnswer ${answer} mapped to UserId ${UserId}: ${utils.getErrorMessage(err)}`)
data_flow: The `answer` parameter (plaintext security answer, used as a password-equivalent credential for account recovery) is interpolated directly into the logger.error call at line 1412. If SecurityAnswerModel.create() throws, the raw answer is written to application logs.
exploit_scenario: An attacker who gains read access to application log files (via LFI, log management system compromise, or misconfigured log aggregation) can recover the plaintext security answers for all seeded users. Security answers are reused across sites and are often close relatives of real passwords.
framework_protection: Winston (logger) has no automatic PII redaction. The log entry persists to whatever transports are configured (file, stdout, external SIEM).
fix_recommendation: Remove the raw `answer` from the error message. Log only the UserId and SecurityQuestionId: `logger.error(`Could not insert SecurityAnswer for UserId ${UserId} / QuestionId ${SecurityQuestionId}: ${utils.getErrorMessage(err)}`)`

question: Q2
title: currentUser endpoint leaks password hash via attacker-controlled 'fields' query parameter
data_flow: GET /rest/user/whoami?fields=password — the `fields` parameter is split and reflected directly from the JWT-decoded user object with no allowlist. The `user.data` object contains every column of the UserModel including `password` (bcrypt hash). The challengeUtils call at line 374 confirms this is intentional for the `passwordHashLeakChallenge` but it is a real vulnerability.
exploit_scenario: Authenticated attacker requests `GET /rest/user/whoami?fields=password,totpSecret,role,deluxeToken`. Response includes the bcrypt hash, TOTP secret, role, and deluxe token for the currently authenticated user. The hash can be used for offline cracking. If another IDOR exists, the attacker can enumerate other users' sensitive fields.
framework_protection: No allowlist, no field-level access control. Express passes the query string through without sanitisation.
fix_recommendation: Implement an explicit allowlist of returnable fields: `const ALLOWED_FIELDS = new Set(['id','email','lastLoginIp','profileImage','username'])`. Filter `requestedFields` against this set before accessing `user.data`.

question: Q2
title: getMemories returns all users' memories with full UserModel join, leaking cross-user PII
exact_code:
const memories = await MemoryModel.findAll({ include: [UserModel] })
res.status(200).json({ status: 'success', data: memories })
data_flow: MemoryModel.findAll with include:[UserModel] performs a JOIN returning every memory row plus the full associated UserModel (email, role, password hash, totpSecret, etc.). The entire result is serialised to JSON and returned to any authenticated caller. No per-user filtering is applied.
exploit_scenario: Any authenticated user calls GET /rest/memories. The response includes every other user's memories along with their full UserModel record (email address, profile image path, role, potentially password hash if not excluded by the model's toJSON). An attacker can enumerate all user emails and profile data from this single endpoint.
framework_protection: Sequelize does not auto-strip sensitive attributes from associated models. `UserModel.toJSON()` must explicitly exclude fields, or `attributes: { exclude: [...] }` must be specified.
fix_recommendation: Filter results to the requesting user only, or apply `attributes: { exclude: ['password','totpSecret','deluxeToken'] }` to the UserModel include. Add a `where: { UserId: req.body.UserId }` clause to scope results.

question: Q2
title: appConfiguration endpoint exposes full server config object to any caller
data_flow: The `config` object from the `config` package is serialised entirely and returned at GET /rest/admin/application-configuration. This object includes application secrets, SMTP credentials, OAuth client IDs, database connection strings, or any other values loaded from config files and environment variable overrides.
exploit_scenario: An unauthenticated (or low-privilege) attacker requests /rest/admin/application-configuration and receives the complete runtime configuration including any credentials or API keys embedded in config files. This provides a pivot point for further infrastructure compromise.
framework_protection: No authentication middleware is referenced in this route handler. No config key allowlist is enforced.
fix_recommendation: Return only a minimal subset of safe, public-facing config values. Apply authentication and authorisation middleware. Never expose the entire `config` object to API consumers.

question: Q5
title: JWT session cookie set without HttpOnly, Secure, or SameSite flags
exact_code: res.cookie('token', token)
data_flow: In `updateAuthenticatedUsers()` (line 1720), the JWT token is written to a cookie using `res.cookie('token', token)` with no options object. This means the cookie is: (1) readable by JavaScript (no HttpOnly), enabling XSS-based session theft; (2) sent over HTTP as well as HTTPS (no Secure flag); (3) sent on cross-origin requests (no SameSite flag), enabling CSRF.
exploit_scenario: An XSS payload (of which Juice Shop has many) calls `document.cookie` and exfiltrates the JWT token. The attacker replays it to authenticate as the victim. Alternatively, a CSRF attack triggers state-changing requests using the automatically-sent cookie.
framework_protection: Express `res.cookie` does not apply any security flags by default. The application must opt in explicitly.
fix_recommendation: Set `res.cookie('token', token, { httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'strict' })`. Additionally, consider using the Authorization header exclusively rather than cookies to eliminate CSRF risk entirely.

question: Q6
title: SecurityAnswer stored and logged in plaintext — no column-level encryption
exact_code: return await SecurityAnswerModel.create({ SecurityQuestionId, UserId, answer })
data_flow: Security answers are inserted into the database as plaintext strings. The model definition does not show any hashing or encryption transform. Combined with the logger finding (DATA-001), the answer is both stored in cleartext and logged in error paths.
exploit_scenario: A SQL injection or direct database access yields plaintext security answers for all users. These answers are typically low-entropy (mother's maiden name, pet's name) and are often shared across sites, enabling account takeover on external services.
framework_protection: Sequelize has no built-in column encryption. Application-level hashing (bcrypt, argon2) must be applied before persistence.
fix_recommendation: Hash security answers before storage using bcrypt or argon2, the same way passwords should be handled. On verification, compare the hash rather than the plaintext. This eliminates both the log exposure risk and the database exposure risk.

question: Q4
title: XXE file disclosure via libxml with entity expansion enabled
exact_code: const xmlDoc = vm.runInContext('libxml.parseXml(data, { noblanks: true, noent: true, nocdata: true })', sandbox, { timeout: 2000 })
data_flow: Uploaded .xml file (POST /file-upload, no auth required) → buffer.toString() → libxml.parseXml with noent:true (external entity resolution enabled) → xmlDoc.toString() appended to error response body
exploit_scenario: Attacker uploads XML with payload: <!DOCTYPE x [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">]><root>&xxe;</root>. With noent:true, libxml resolves the entity, reads the file, and returns its contents in the HTTP 410 error response. Achieves arbitrary local file read with no authentication.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Parse XML with noent:false to disable external entity resolution. Consider rejecting XML uploads entirely since the route already returns HTTP 410.

question: Q4
title: Server-Side Template Injection and LFR via req.body spread into Handlebars render context
data_flow: req.body.layout (POST /dataerasure, authenticated) → path.resolve() → blocklist check (easily bypassed with path traversal or case tricks) → passed as Handlebars layout path; entire req.body spread into template context
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends layout=/etc/passwd — the blocklist only checks for 'ftp', 'ctf.key', 'encryptionkeys' substrings, so /etc/passwd passes. Handlebars attempts to render /etc/passwd as a layout, and the first 100 chars of the result are returned (the sendlfrResponse slice). Additionally, arbitrary req.body keys become Handlebars template variables, enabling prototype pollution of the template context.
framework_protection: partial
fix_recommendation: Replace blocklist with an explicit allowlist of permitted layout names (e.g., only 'default'). Never spread req.body into render context — explicitly name each allowed property.

question: Q5
title: Open redirect bypass via substring matching in isRedirectAllowed
exact_code: allowed = allowed || url.includes(allowedUrl)
data_flow: req.query.to (GET /redirect?to=...) → isRedirectAllowed() checks if toUrl.includes() any allowlisted URL as substring → res.redirect(toUrl) if allowed
exploit_scenario: The check uses includes() not startsWith(). Attacker sends: GET /redirect?to=https://evil.com/?x=https://github.com/juice-shop/juice-shop — this passes because the URL string includes the allowlisted GitHub URL. User is redirected to evil.com, enabling phishing.
framework_protection: bypassed
fix_recommendation: Use url.startsWith(allowedUrl) with normalized URL comparison, or maintain an exact allowlist set and require equality. The isUnintendedRedirect function already uses startsWith correctly — isRedirectAllowed should match.

question: Q2
title: AdmissionReceiptChain and write storage are INDEPENDENT ledgers with no cross-reference enforced
finding: The AdmissionReceiptChain is an in-memory list (_receipt_chain = AdmissionReceiptChain() at module level). The receipt is created and appended to the chain at Step 5 of the write route BEFORE the database upsert (Step 7) and _persist_memory (Step 8). If the adapter.upsert() or _persist_memory() call fails, the exception is caught by the broad except Exception block at line 288 which raises HTTPException(500). The receipt has already been appended to _receipt_chain with decision=ADMITTED at that point. There is NO rollback of the in-memory receipt chain entry on write failure. Result: a receipt with ADMITTED exists in the chain for a memory_id that was never persisted to the database or vector store. A replay check on that receipt_hash would return is_legal=True even though the memory was never actually written. Conversely, there is no check on the write path that a receipt must exist before storage is attempted — these two ledgers are tracked independently.
exact_lines:
# Step 5: Create admission receipt
receipt = _receipt_chain.append(
    memory_id=memory_id,
    actor_id=request.actor.id,
    ...
    admission_decision=AdmissionDecision.ADMITTED,
)

# Step 7: Upsert to vector DB
adapter = get_adapter()
vector_id = f"vec_{memory_id}"
await adapter.upsert(vector_id, embedding, metadata)

# Step 8: Persist to document store (SQLite)
await _persist_memory(...)

...
except Exception as e:
    logger.exception("v1.memory.write.error", extra={"audit_ref": audit_ref})
    raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail="Internal error")
exploit_scenario: An attacker who can cause adapter.upsert() to fail (e.g., via a flaky or throttled vector DB) will end up with ADMITTED receipts in the chain for memories that do not exist in storage. The audit chain then falsely attests to admissions that were never completed. A forensic replay will declare is_legal=True for phantom memories.

question: Q5
title: Recall path skips ALL content-level admission gates — only permission tier is checked
finding: The v1_recall route applies only _check_actor_permission(request.actor.role, ActorRole.T2_READ) before querying the vector DB and returning results. It does NOT call _validate_admission(), does NOT run the content firewall (scan_content), does NOT run the forbidden-key scan, and does NOT apply any omega rule check against the recalled content. The query text (request.query.text) is fed directly to _generate_embedding() without any sanitization or gate check. Data returned from the vector DB is returned directly to the caller. If content was written that bypassed admission gates (e.g., via the ImportError firewall bypass in Q1), it would be retrievable by any T2_READ actor. Furthermore, the query intent (request.intent.use_case, request.intent.description) is only logged — it is never validated or enforced programmatically.
exact_lines:
# Step 1: Check permission
_check_actor_permission(request.actor.role, ActorRole.T2_READ)

# Step 2: Validate intent (log for audit)
logger.info(
    "v1.memory.recall.intent",
    extra={
        "actor_id": request.actor.id,
        "use_case": request.intent.use_case,
        "description": request.intent.description,
        ...
    },
)

# Step 3: Get query embedding
if request.query.embedding:
    query_embedding = request.query.embedding
else:
    query_embedding = await _generate_embedding(request.query.text)
exploit_scenario: A T2_READ actor supplies a query with a declared intent of 'compliance-review' but probes for sensitive data by crafting embeddings that semantically match forbidden content. The intent field is never validated — any string is accepted. Separately, if an attacker provides a raw pre-computed embedding in request.query.embedding, the system uses it directly with zero sanitization, bypassing even the embedding generation step.

question: Q7
title: Validator chain in validators.py is exhaustive (must-all-pass) but Gate 2 (content firewall) in v1 route has a silent ImportError bypass
finding: In enforcement/validators.py, the validate_memory_write() function runs three gates sequentially with fail-closed semantics: (1) Pydantic schema validation raises MemoryError on failure; (1.5) content firewall raises MemoryError on violation; (2) forbidden-key scan raises MemoryError on violation. There is no short-circuit path — all gates must pass. However, the v1 route's _validate_admission() function (a different, parallel validation implementation) has an explicit try/except ImportError around Gate 2 (content firewall) that silently passes if the module is unavailable. This means the v1 route's validator chain is NOT exhaustive — it is conditionally exhaustive depending on import availability. The validators.py chain itself is sound, but the v1 route bypasses it entirely and uses its own lighter chain.
exploit_scenario: The v1 route never calls validators.py's validate_memory_write() — it has a parallel, weaker implementation in _validate_admission(). If enforcement.content_firewall is absent or removed, the entire firewall gate silently disappears from the v1 write path. The v1 route's Gate 1 (string pattern match) and Gate 3 (omega rule prefix) still run, but these are far weaker than the full content firewall.

question: Q2
title: Model calibration thresholds pinned to Feb 2026 — now 3+ weeks old with no upgrade flag
finding: MEMORY.md documents that 'All empirical thresholds are pinned to model versions as of Feb 2026. Must re-calibrate on model upgrades.' The current date is 2026-03-08. No explicit model version tags are stored — there is no record of which specific model version hash/release the thresholds were calibrated against. Without version pinning, it is impossible to determine whether the models currently in use match the calibrated versions or whether a silent upgrade has occurred that requires re-calibration. The warning exists but is operationally incomplete.
exact_quote:
"All empirical thresholds are pinned to model versions as of Feb 2026. Must re-calibrate on model upgrades."
"Confidence ranges per model (Gemini 0.70-1.00, Codex 0.88-0.99, Claude 0.90-0.95)"
recommendation: Add explicit model version/release identifiers to the calibration block (e.g., 'Calibrated against: Claude claude-sonnet-4-6, Gemini gemini-2.0-flash, Codex codex-XXXX as of 2026-02-18'). Without version pinning, the re-calibration warning cannot be acted on. Also add a session-start check recommendation: run `--calibrate` flag on SAP Relay Runner at the start of any high-stakes multi-model run.

question: Q4
title: MEMORY.md SAP v1.2 section is 12 lines of dense detail without a topic file link
finding: The '## SAP v1.2 — Implemented' section in MEMORY.md spans 20+ lines including: validator path, confidence floor change, three-state tier details, gaps[] field specification, lint check 7 details, Codex ID namespace, with-canary macro, full Model Behavior Calibration sub-section with 5 bullet points, and a backlog sub-section. This is a full technical spec embedded in an index file. It links to 'sap-v1.2-backlog.md' (which is in the orchestration directory, not the memory directory) but has no corresponding topic file in the memory directory.
exact_quote:
"## SAP v1.2 — Implemented (2026-02-18)
- **Validator**: ...
- **Confidence floor**: 0.5 → 0.6...
- **Three-state tier**: REJECT (<0.6) / CONJECTURE (0.6-0.75) / VERIFIED (>0.75)...
### Model Behavior Calibration
All empirical thresholds are pinned..."
recommendation: Create a 'sap-protocol.md' topic file in the memory directory (or verify sap-benchmarks.md can absorb this). Move the full SAP v1.2 implementation details there. Replace the MEMORY.md section with a 3-line summary: version, validator path, calibration warning, and 'See sap-benchmarks.md for thresholds and benchmarks.'

question: Q5
title: Broken cross-reference: early-prompts-index.md links outside memory directory
finding: MEMORY.md contains '## Early Prompts Collection — See [early-prompts-index.md](~/.claude/orchestration/early-prompts-index.md)'. The target path is outside the memory directory (it points to ~/.claude/orchestration/early-prompts-index.md), and this file is NOT one of the 8 memory topic files listed in the audit scope. The link also uses an absolute tilde path rather than a relative path, making it non-functional as a markdown hyperlink.
exact_quote: "## Early Prompts Collection — See [early-prompts-index.md](~/.claude/orchestration/early-prompts-index.md)"
recommendation: Verify that ~/.claude/orchestration/early-prompts-index.md exists (filesystem listing confirms it does). Since it exists but is outside the memory directory, update the MEMORY.md reference to use an absolute path and add a note that it is in the orchestration directory, not the memory directory. Consider whether early-prompts-index.md should be copied/linked into the memory directory for completeness.

question: Q6
title: genome-visualizer.md is a topic file with no MEMORY.md index entry
finding: genome-visualizer.md is one of the 8 memory topic files and contains substantial project knowledge about the Human Chromosome Visualizer (view architecture, LOD system, SNP filtering, chromosome switching). However, MEMORY.md has NO dedicated section or link for this file. The Active Projects table mentions 'Human Chromosome Visualizer' but does not reference genome-visualizer.md by name or link.
exact_quote:
MEMORY.md Active Projects table: "| Human Chromosome Visualizer | 3D interactive DNA explorer, local-only, format-agnostic, funds infra project | ~90% |"
[No 'See genome-visualizer.md' link anywhere in MEMORY.md]
recommendation: Add a section to MEMORY.md: '## Human Chromosome Visualizer — See [genome-visualizer.md](genome-visualizer.md)' with a 2-3 line summary of the key gotchas (ViewManager overlay architecture, LOD guard pattern, SNP scale-to-zero). Update the Active Projects table entry to include the link.

question: Q7
title: Model-role fitness matrix has calibration warning but no model version identifiers
finding: MEMORY.md stores 'Model-role fitness matrix (Gemini 0% adversarial, Codex 9/10 arithmetic)' as empirical data and includes the note 'Must re-calibrate on model upgrades.' However, no specific model versions are recorded (e.g., Gemini 2.0 Flash, Claude claude-sonnet-4-6, Codex specific release). Without version anchors, the re-calibration trigger — 'on model upgrades' — cannot be acted upon because there is no baseline to compare against. You cannot know if a model was upgraded without knowing what version was in use when calibration ran.
exact_quote:
"All empirical thresholds are pinned to model versions as of Feb 2026. **Must re-calibrate on model upgrades.**
- Confidence floor (0.6), conjecture threshold (0.75) — derived from observed ranges (min 0.88 legitimate)
- Model-role fitness matrix (Gemini 0% adversarial, Codex 9/10 arithmetic) — lint check 7
- Confidence ranges per model (Gemini 0.70-1.00, Codex 0.88-0.99, Claude 0.90-0.95)"
recommendation: Record explicit model version strings alongside each calibration data point. Minimum: add a table row to model-profiles.json in the sap-relay skill that captures (model_name, model_version, calibration_date, threshold_values). The --calibrate flag exists in SAP Relay Runner but the model-profiles.json baseline should include version info so drift detection can confirm whether a version change triggered the need for re-calibration.

question: Q1
title: errorhandler middleware active unconditionally — full stack traces exposed to clients
exact_code:
const errorhandler = require('errorhandler')
...
app.use(verify.errorHandlingChallenge())
app.use(errorhandler())
...
errorhandler.title = `${config.get<string>('application.name')} (Express ${utils.version('express')})`
data_flow: Any unhandled Express error → verify.errorHandlingChallenge() (challenge bookkeeping only) → errorhandler() → sends full HTML error page including stack trace, file paths, and Express/Node version to the HTTP client. The errorhandler package is explicitly designed for development use and its own README warns it must never be used in production.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker sends a malformed JSON body or triggers any route that calls next(err). 2. errorhandler responds with an HTML page containing the full Node.js stack trace, absolute file paths on disk, internal module names, and the Express version string. 3. The test in httpSpec.ts line 45 confirms this: a GET to /rest/x returns status 500 with '<title>Error: Unexpected path: /rest/x</title>' and a full stack trace body. 4. This information directly aids path-traversal, dependency-version fingerprinting, and targeted exploit selection.
framework_protection: errorhandler should only be loaded when process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development'. In production Express apps the correct pattern is a four-argument (err, req, res, next) middleware that logs internally and returns a generic 500 response with no internal detail.
fix_recommendation: Wrap the errorhandler() call: if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development') { app.use(errorhandler()); } else { app.use((err, req, res, next) => { logger.error(err); res.status(500).json({ status: 'error', message: 'An unexpected error occurred.' }); }); }

question: Q3
title: CORS wildcard allows all origins — no origin restriction
data_flow: The cors() middleware with no configuration sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on every response. The test in httpSpec.ts line 15 explicitly asserts this: 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *'.
