failure_mode: The root endpoint always advertises 'docs': '/docs' even when CMV1_ENABLE_DOCS=false disables the actual /docs route. This is minor but misleads clients and leaks the configuration intent.

title: No CORS middleware applied when CMV1_CORS_ORIGINS is unset — browser CORS may be entirely open
lines: 116-126: _cors_origins = os.getenv('CMV1_CORS_ORIGINS', '') / allowed_origins = [...] / if allowed_origins: / app.add_middleware(CORSMiddleware, ...)
failure_mode: If CMV1_CORS_ORIGINS is not set, no CORSMiddleware is registered at all. This means the browser's same-origin policy is the only control — the service emits no CORS headers. Depending on deployment context (reverse proxy stripping headers, or CORS being needed for legitimate cross-origin UI), this can result in either open CORS (if a proxy adds permissive headers) or broken UI. No security headers (X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, HSTS, CSP) are set by any middleware in either case.

title: No security response headers set
lines: (no lines — absence finding)
failure_mode: Neither app_prod.py nor any visible middleware sets X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, Content-Security-Policy, or Referrer-Policy. This is relevant if the service is accessed from browser-facing contexts or the /docs endpoint is enabled.

title: Auth key logged to stdout (not stderr) in production — key count leaks to application log
lines: 650: print(f'Production mode: {len(provided_keys)} API key(s) configured')
failure_mode: All fatal/warning messages in initialize_auth() correctly write to sys.stderr. Line 650 uses plain print() which goes to stdout. In containerized deployments stdout is typically captured as application log (e.g., Docker JSON log driver, CloudWatch, ECS). The count is low-value, but this is an inconsistency that should be noted as it sets a precedent — a future developer adding the actual key value here instead of count would be a Critical finding.

title: Dev-mode generated API key printed to stderr — captured by log aggregators in shared environments
lines: 583: print(f'    {random_key}', file=sys.stderr)
failure_mode: In CMV1_DEV_MODE=true, the generated API key is printed verbatim to stderr. The code itself warns 'Keys printed here may appear in logs' (line 589). In practice: container orchestrators (Kubernetes, ECS, Docker Compose) capture both stdout and stderr to log aggregation systems (Datadog, Splunk, CloudWatch). Anyone with log-read access can extract the session key. The 'dev mode' label does not prevent this from being deployed to shared or semi-production environments.

title: Weak key check is a warning only — short keys (< 16 chars) accepted in production
lines: 631-648: if len(key) < 16: / weak_keys.append(key) / ... / # Warning only, don't exit
failure_mode: Keys shorter than 16 characters are accepted in production with only a stderr warning. The KNOWN_DEV_KEYS blocklist covers common short strings but not all weak short keys. A 16-character minimum is itself weak for a production API key — secrets.token_urlsafe(32) produces ~43 chars. The fail-closed guarantee is partially undermined since callers can use legitimately short but non-blocklisted keys.

title: Constant-time comparison is linear over the key set — timing oracle on key count
lines: 707-710: key_valid = any( / secrets.compare_digest(api_key, valid_key) / for valid_key in VALID_API_KEYS / )
failure_mode: secrets.compare_digest is used correctly per key, but any() short-circuits on the first True match. If there is exactly one key, this is fine. With multiple keys, the number of comparisons performed varies based on which position in the set the valid key occupies. Python set iteration order is deterministic within a process (hash-randomized across restarts), but the total iteration count leaks via timing: a valid key matched at position N takes N * O(len(key)) time, an invalid key always takes the full K iterations. An attacker with many trials and timing precision could infer set size and potentially position. Low practical exploitability, but defeats the intent of constant-time comparison.

title: Invalid API key value logged as actor_id in audit log — partial key oracle
lines: 714-715: log_auth_failure( / actor_id=api_key, ...
failure_mode: When authentication fails, the submitted (invalid) api_key is passed verbatim as actor_id to log_auth_failure(). If the audit log is accessible to operators and the attacker is probing with near-valid keys (one character off), the logged value gives them confirmation of what was submitted. More critically, if a valid key is accidentally submitted to the wrong endpoint or with a typo in the header name, the correct key is now in the audit log. The actor_id parameter for auth failures should be masked or hashed before logging.

title: KNOWN_DEV_KEYS blocklist is a static allowlist — does not cover all predictable/weak patterns
lines: 493-516: KNOWN_DEV_KEYS: FrozenSet[str] = frozenset({...})
failure_mode: The blocklist covers common string literals but is enumeration-based, not pattern-based. Keys like 'production-key', 'my-api-key-v1', 'cmv1-prod-key', 'service-account-key' pass all checks. A minimum-entropy requirement (e.g., checking against a dictionary or requiring cryptographic randomness verification) would be stronger. The current approach provides false confidence that any key not on the list is secure.

title: Transcript patterns lack IGNORECASE flag — design doc requires re.IGNORECASE
failure_mode: All transcript patterns are compiled with re.MULTILINE only. The design doc example code explicitly uses re.IGNORECASE: `re.compile(r'(User|Human)\s*:\s*.+?(Assistant|AI)\s*:', re.IGNORECASE)`. Without IGNORECASE, payloads using 'user:', 'ASSISTANT:', 'assistant:', 'USER:', 'human:', 'claude:' (any mixed-case variant except the exact literals in patterns) will bypass detection entirely. For example, 'user: what is my password?\nassistant: hunter2' evades all transcript patterns.
design_gap: FIXES_FROM_EVALUATION_V2.md line 133: `re.compile(r'(User|Human)\s*:\s*.+?(Assistant|AI)\s*:', re.IGNORECASE)` — the reference implementation in the design doc includes IGNORECASE; the shipped code does not.

title: Default scan() covers only 'summary' and 'justification' — other persisted fields never scanned
lines:
if fields is None:
    fields = ['summary', 'justification']
failure_mode: The repository write projection persists: id, class_toggle, summary, justification, provenance, references, tags, timestamps, internal metadata. The firewall's default field list covers only summary and justification. Fields 'provenance', 'references', and 'tags' are persisted but never scanned by default. An attacker can embed a raw transcript or base64 blob in the 'references' array or 'provenance' string and it will pass the firewall unchecked. callers that do not explicitly pass a custom fields list (including the validators.py orchestrator, which is described as the integration point) will silently skip these fields.
design_gap: component-analysis.md line 105: 'Fires on the summary and justification fields.' — this is documented as the intended scope, but architecture-analysis.md line 28 states 'every write path passes through policy enforcement before reaching storage' which implies full coverage, not just two fields.

title: Multi-field split-payload attack: transcript distributed across summary + justification evades detection
failure_mode: Fields are scanned independently with no cross-field concatenation. An attacker can split a transcript so that no single field alone triggers a pattern: summary='User: What is my password?' (no Assistant: follow-up in same field) and justification='Assistant: hunter2' (no User: prefix in same field). The transcript_multi_turn pattern requires both User: and Assistant: in the same string. The individual prefix patterns (transcript_user_prefix, transcript_newline_user) would fire on each field separately — BUT only if the content starts with or contains those prefixes. 'User: What is my password?' in summary alone DOES match transcript_user_prefix, so this specific split is caught. However, splitting at line boundaries or using variants that individually look benign while together form a full transcript is still exploitable.

title: BASE64_PATTERN and HEX_PATTERN require full-line match — embedded/wrapped encoding evades detection
failure_mode: Both patterns use anchored full-line matching (^ and $) and require the entire stripped line to consist of base64/hex characters. This means: (1) A base64 blob preceded by any non-base64 text like 'data: SGVsbG8gV29ybGQ...' on the same line passes undetected. (2) Base64 wrapped in JSON ('"content": "SGVsbG8..."') passes because the line contains quotes and a key. (3) Hex strings embedded mid-sentence pass. (4) Base64 split across multiple lines in chunks of 49 chars each (below the 50-char minimum) pass entirely. (5) URL-safe base64 using '-' and '_' instead of '+' and '/' is not covered at all.
design_gap: Module docstring line 8: 'Base64-encoded data' listed as a detection target. FIXES_FROM_EVALUATION_V2.md line 103-110 shows the motivating example uses a JSON-wrapped payload, yet the base64 detector will miss base64 embedded in JSON values.

title: Entropy check skips fields shorter than 100 characters — short encoded payloads bypass entirely
failure_mode: Any field with fewer than 100 characters skips entropy detection entirely. A base64-encoded secret, API key, or short conversation fragment under 100 chars will never trigger HIGH_ENTROPY_CONTENT. For context, a 64-byte secret (the minimum HEX_PATTERN detects) encodes to ~88 base64 characters — under the entropy minimum. Since BASE64_PATTERN also requires a full clean line, a short base64 blob in a mixed-content field shorter than 100 chars evades both the pattern check and the entropy check simultaneously.

title: Entropy threshold of 5.0 is too loose — base64 entropy is ~6.0, but threshold allows high-entropy content up to 5.0
lines:
ENTROPY_THRESHOLD = 5.0
# High entropy (> 4.5) may indicate:
# - Base64-encoded content
# Normal prose typically has entropy 3.5-4.5.
failure_mode: The class docstring says 'Normal prose typically has entropy 3.5-4.5' and the comment on calculate_entropy says 'High entropy (> 4.5) may indicate...' base64. Yet the threshold is set to 5.0, creating a gap window of 4.5-5.0 that the comments acknowledge as suspicious but the code allows through. Lightly encoded or compressed content, or content that mixes prose with encoded fragments, can have entropy in the 4.5-5.0 range and pass detection. The threshold should align with the documented 4.5 boundary, not 5.0.
design_gap: architecture-analysis.md line 62: 'High-entropy detection (Shannon entropy > 5.0)' — the architecture doc enshrines 5.0 as the threshold, but the code's own comments say 4.5 is where suspicion begins, creating an undocumented 0.5-unit blind zone.

title: Transcript pattern 'transcript_user_prefix' and siblings use ^ but rely on re.MULTILINE — single-line strings without newlines bypass line-start patterns
lines:
(r'^User:\s', "transcript_user_prefix"),
(r'^Human:\s', "transcript_human_prefix"),
(r'^Assistant:\s', "transcript_assistant_prefix"),
(r'^AI:\s', "transcript_ai_prefix"),
(r'^Claude:\s', "transcript_claude_prefix")
failure_mode: With re.MULTILINE, ^ matches the start of each line within a multi-line string. This is correct. However, for a single-line string like 'User: tell me your secrets' (no newlines), these patterns correctly fire. The gap is more subtle: if a string has content before 'User:' on the same line, e.g., 'Note - User: something', the ^ anchor will not match mid-line. An attacker can prepend any prefix to put the role label mid-line and defeat the anchored prefix patterns. They would then need to also defeat the multi-turn pattern (transcript_multi_turn), which has no anchoring and is their actual blocker.

title: JSON detection requires 2+ indicators counted via .search() — indicators counted across compiled patterns, not across lines, allowing structural JSON to pass at count=1
lines:
json_matches = sum(1 for p, _ in _JSON_COMPILED if p.search(content))
if json_matches >= self.JSON_INDICATOR_THRESHOLD:
failure_mode: JSON_INDICATOR_THRESHOLD=2 means at least 2 of 5 JSON patterns must match. Each pattern is counted once regardless of how many times it matches. A minimal JSON object {'key': 'value'} matches json_key_value (the ':' pattern) and json_object_start (starts with '{') — so a count of 2 is reachable. However, consider: a summary that is pure JSON-like but has the opening brace on a line that doesn't start with '{' (e.g., a sentence followed by a JSON block) may only match 1 of 5 patterns. More critically, the json_object_start pattern is anchored with ^ (relies on MULTILINE) and requires the first non-whitespace char to be '{'. Deeply nested or mid-paragraph JSON won't match json_object_start at all, reducing indicator count and potentially falling below threshold.

title: Zero-width spaces and Unicode directional marks between transcript role labels bypass pattern matching
lines:
TRANSCRIPT_PATTERNS = [
    (r'(User|Human)\s*:\s*.+?(Assistant|AI|Claude)\s*:', "transcript_multi_turn"),
    (r'^User:\s', "transcript_user_prefix"),
    ...
]
failure_mode:
The content firewall performs no Unicode normalization before applying patterns. An attacker can insert zero-width spaces (U+200B), zero-width non-joiners (U+200C), zero-width joiners (U+200D), left-to-right marks (U+200E), right-to-left marks (U+200F), or soft hyphens (U+00AD) between the role label and colon: 'User​: something
Assistant‌: response'. The \s* between 'User' and ':' in the multi-turn pattern only matches whitespace characters (space, tab, newline, etc.) — not zero-width characters. This is particularly ironic given that lexical_scanner.py is documented as having 20+ zero-width character mappings for key scanning, but content_firewall.py has none.
design_gap: component-analysis.md line 110: 'Zero-width character detection (20+ characters catalogued)' — this hardening exists only in the lexical_scanner for keys, not in content_firewall for values. The design doc describes them as 'complementary' but the zero-width hardening is not applied symmetrically.

title: HTML entity encoding and Unicode escapes in content bypass all pattern matching
lines:
TRANSCRIPT_PATTERNS = [...]
JSON_PATTERNS = [...]
XML_PATTERNS = [...]
failure_mode: No HTML entity decoding or Unicode escape processing occurs before pattern matching. Content like 'User: hello\nAssistant: world' (HTML entity encoded) or 'U\u0073er: hello' (Unicode escape for 's') will not match any transcript pattern. Similarly, JSON content with Unicode-escaped braces ('\u007b\u0022key\u0022') bypasses JSON_PATTERNS. The module docstring claims to detect 'Base64-encoded data' broadly but there is no decoding step before detection.
design_gap: Module docstring lines 6-11 list detection targets including 'Base64-encoded data' but there is no pre-processing decode step. An attacker who encodes their transcript payload in HTML entities or Unicode escapes defeats every pattern in the module.

title: scan() silently skips non-string field values — list and dict fields in 'references' and 'tags' never inspected
lines:
content = data[field_name]
if isinstance(content, str):
    fields_scanned += 1
    violations = self.scan_field(field_name, content)
failure_mode: If a field value is a list (e.g., 'references': ['User: what is my password?', 'Assistant: hunter2']) or a dict, the isinstance(content, str) check causes it to be silently skipped. The field is not counted in fields_scanned either, so the caller has no indication that non-string fields were ignored. Transcript content nested inside a list value in a field that is also in the scan list will pass undetected. This is particularly relevant for 'references' and 'tags' which are likely to be list-typed.

title: scan_field() has no exception handling — any regex error or unexpected input causes an unhandled exception that propagates to caller
failure_mode: If pattern.search(content) raises (e.g., due to catastrophic backtracking on a crafted input, or a re module internal error), the exception propagates uncaught through scan_field() and scan(). There is no try/except wrapping the scan loop. Depending on how the caller (validators.py) handles exceptions, this could result in fail-open behavior where a crafted payload causes the firewall to throw an exception, the exception is caught at the route layer, and the write proceeds without a firewall decision. The re module can experience catastrophic backtracking: the transcript_multi_turn pattern `(User|Human)\s*:\s*.+?(Assistant|AI|Claude)\s*:` with a very long string that nearly matches could cause O(n^2) or worse backtracking.
design_gap: component-analysis.md line 28: 'every write path passes through policy enforcement before reaching storage' — an exception in the firewall that is caught at a higher layer and results in a 500 error without blocking the write would violate this invariant.

title: Catastrophic backtracking risk in transcript_multi_turn pattern
lines: (r'(User|Human)\s*:\s*.+?(Assistant|AI|Claude)\s*:', "transcript_multi_turn")
failure_mode: The pattern uses `.+?` (non-greedy any-char) between two anchoring groups. On a string that contains 'User:' but no subsequent 'Assistant:' (or variant), the regex engine will attempt to match .+? against every possible suffix position before giving up. On long strings (multi-KB summaries), this is O(n) in the best case but can degrade significantly. More critically, if the string contains something like 'User: AAAAAAAAAAAAA...AAAA' (thousands of A's), the engine's backtracking through the non-greedy match can cause severe latency spikes — a potential DoS vector against the hot write path.
design_gap: IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md line 141-144: 'Shannon entropy + regex scanning is effective but expensive. Potential latency bottleneck at scale.' — the plan acknowledges latency risk but attributes it to entropy; the regex catastrophic backtracking risk is unacknowledged.

title: Pre-compiled module-level pattern lists are module globals — no guard if _compile_patterns() returns empty or raises
lines:
_TRANSCRIPT_COMPILED = _compile_patterns(TRANSCRIPT_PATTERNS)
_JSON_COMPILED = _compile_patterns(JSON_PATTERNS)
_XML_COMPILED = _compile_patterns(XML_PATTERNS)
_STACK_COMPILED = _compile_patterns(STACK_PATTERNS)
failure_mode: If _compile_patterns() raises during module import (e.g., due to an invalid regex in the pattern list), the entire module fails to import and all callers fail with ImportError. This is fail-closed at the module level, which is good. However, if patterns were ever modified to return an empty list (e.g., via a bad patch), the for loops in scan_field() would silently iterate over nothing and find no violations. There is no assertion or check that the compiled lists are non-empty. Additionally, re.compile() is not thread-safe with regard to the internal cache on some Python versions, though in practice the pre-compilation at module load time (before any threads start) mitigates this.

title: Log/stack trace detection breaks on first match — only one STACK_COMPILED pattern checked, rest skipped
failure_mode: The break after the first log match means only one LOG_LIKE_CONTENT violation is ever appended per field, regardless of how many patterns match. This is by design (as commented). However, the audit trail loses information: if both a python_traceback and a java_exception are present, only one is reported. For forensic purposes this reduces the detail available in the violation log. More importantly, transcript detection also uses break (line 229) and XML detection also uses break (line 251) — this is consistent behavior but means the violations list understates the severity of a compound attack.

title: Entropy calculation operates on entire field content, not per-line — high-entropy islands diluted by surrounding prose
lines:
if self.detect_high_entropy and len(content) >= self.MIN_ENTROPY_LENGTH:
    entropy = calculate_entropy(content)
failure_mode: Shannon entropy is computed over the entire field string. If a field contains 200 chars of normal English prose followed by a 60-char base64 blob, the overall entropy of the combined 260-char string will be lower than the blob alone, potentially falling under the 5.0 threshold. The blob's high entropy is diluted by the surrounding prose. A per-line entropy check (similar to how base64 is detected line-by-line) or a sliding-window entropy check would catch embedded high-entropy islands. As-implemented, padding a base64 blob with enough English text suppresses the entropy signal.

title: XML detection not subject to indicator threshold — single XML tag triggers violation
failure_mode: JSON detection requires JSON_INDICATOR_THRESHOLD=2 matches to prevent false positives. XML detection fires on the first pattern match with no equivalent threshold. The xml_open_tag pattern `<[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*\s*[^>]*>` is broad enough to match HTML fragments, markdown with angle brackets, or even natural language descriptions of XML (e.g., 'the <config> section'). This creates asymmetric false-positive risk. More significantly, the inverse is also a problem: an attacker who knows the threshold is 1 for XML has no way to dilute below it, but the inconsistency suggests the XML threshold may have been an oversight rather than intentional design.

title: HEX_PATTERN minimum length of 64 chars misses shorter hex-encoded secrets and keys
lines: HEX_PATTERN = re.compile(r'^[0-9a-fA-F]{64,}$')
failure_mode: The minimum of 64 hex characters corresponds to a 32-byte (256-bit) value, which is a reasonable minimum for detecting SHA-256 hashes or AES-256 keys. However, many secrets are shorter: MD5 hashes are 32 hex chars, SHA-1 hashes are 40 hex chars, 16-byte (128-bit) keys are 32 hex chars, and many API tokens are encoded as 32-48 hex characters. All of these fall below the 64-char minimum and pass undetected.

title: scan() does not normalize content before scanning — leading/trailing whitespace, tab-expansion, and line-ending variants not handled
lines:
content = data[field_name]
if isinstance(content, str):
    fields_scanned += 1
    violations = self.scan_field(field_name, content)
failure_mode:
Content is passed directly to scan_field() without any normalization. Windows-style line endings (
) may interfere with patterns that match 
 specifically. The transcript_newline_user pattern `
(User|Human):\s` will not match '
User: ' because  is consumed before the 
. An attacker who controls line endings can force Windows-style 
 into the payload to defeat newline-anchored patterns on a Linux host.

title: FirewallResult.fields_scanned counts only string-typed fields, misleading callers about coverage
failure_mode: fields_scanned is incremented only when the field value is a string. If a caller passes fields=['summary', 'references', 'tags'] and references/tags contain lists (not strings), fields_scanned=1 but the caller sees 1 and may assume 1 of 3 fields were scanned when in fact the coverage was 1 of 3 requested. There is no 'fields_skipped' or 'non_string_fields' counter in FirewallResult. A monitoring system relying on fields_scanned to verify firewall coverage will be misled.

title: No logging at any level — firewall violations are returned but never logged
failure_mode: The firewall produces ContentViolation objects but does not log them. There is no import of logging in the module. Violations are only visible if the caller explicitly inspects the returned FirewallResult. If the caller logs the violation, it is dependent on caller discipline. A bypass or repeated probing pattern against the firewall will leave no trace in system logs unless the upper layer (validators.py or route handler) explicitly logs each FirewallResult. Passive monitoring of firewall activity, attack pattern analysis, and anomaly detection are all impossible at the firewall layer.
design_gap: architecture-analysis.md line 28: 'every modification is an auditable event' — the firewall itself produces no audit events. Auditability is delegated entirely to callers.

title: calculate_entropy() returns 0.0 for empty string — correct, but single-character strings return 0.0 (not caught by MIN_ENTROPY_LENGTH guard for very short strings)
failure_mode: For a single-character string ('A'), freq={'A':1}, prob=1.0, entropy = -(1.0 * log2(1.0)) = -(1.0 * 0.0) = 0.0. This is mathematically correct. For a string of all identical characters ('AAAA...'), entropy is also 0.0. This is handled correctly and will not cause false-negatives (0.0 < 5.0 threshold). The guard `len(content) >= MIN_ENTROPY_LENGTH` also prevents this from being a practical issue. No actual bug, but the edge case is worth noting for test coverage.

title: ContentFirewall constructor allows all detection checks to be disabled via parameters — no enforcement that at least one check is active
lines:
def __init__(
    self,
    *,
    detect_transcripts: bool = True,
    detect_raw_output: bool = True,
    detect_encoded: bool = True,
    detect_high_entropy: bool = True,
    detect_logs: bool = True,
    entropy_threshold: float = 5.0,
):
failure_mode: A caller can instantiate ContentFirewall(detect_transcripts=False, detect_raw_output=False, detect_encoded=False, detect_high_entropy=False, detect_logs=False) and receive a firewall that always returns clean=True. There is no validation that at least one detection mode is enabled, no assertion on entropy_threshold range (e.g., entropy_threshold=0.0 would flag everything; entropy_threshold=100.0 would flag nothing). The module-level _default_firewall uses all defaults (all checks on, threshold=5.0), so the convenience functions are safe. But any caller who constructs their own instance can silently disable the firewall entirely.

question: Q1
title: Hash computed AFTER validation — hash does not cover data mutation risk, but ordering is sound for idempotency
finding: In the write route, _validate_admission() runs at Step 2 and _compute_content_hash() runs at Step 3. The validation fires first, so a rejected payload never reaches the hash step. However, this means the stored content_hash is computed from the raw (pre-embed, post-validation) request object — not from a canonicalized or sanitized copy. If validation enriches or mutates the request object in memory (it does not currently, but the architecture permits it), the hash would cover the enriched form while the receipt covers the original. More concretely: the v1 _validate_admission() applies a soft firewall gate (Gate 2) that silently ignores ImportError; if that gate is absent due to import failure, content that would have been rejected passes validation, gets a hash, and is stored — but the hash was computed before the firewall would have altered anything, so the hash is consistent with the admitted payload. The real risk is the ImportError swallow.
exact_lines:
# Step 2: Validate admission (forbidden keys scan)
violations = await asyncio.to_thread(_validate_admission, request)
if violations:
    ...
    raise HTTPException(...)

# Step 3: Idempotency check
content_hash = _compute_content_hash(request)
---
# Gate 2: Content firewall (same as v2)
try:
    from enforcement.content_firewall import scan_content
    firewall_result = scan_content(...)
    ...
except ImportError:
    # Content firewall not available - continue without it
    pass
exploit_scenario: If the enforcement.content_firewall module is unavailable at runtime (ImportError silently swallowed), Gate 2 is entirely skipped. Forbidden raw-context content passes the content firewall check, proceeds to hash computation and storage. The attacker could deliberately break the import (e.g., by removing the module) to disable the firewall gate while the other gates still appear to run.

question: Q3
title: Embedding cache key is NOT used in any filesystem path construction — no path traversal risk
finding: In embed_cache.py, the _key() function builds a structured cache key in the format '{provider}:{model}:{dim}:{spec_version}:{text_hash}' where the text component is always SHA-256 hashed before inclusion. This key is used only as a dictionary key in an in-memory dict (load_cache returns a Dict, lookup_batch indexes into it). The cache file path itself is passed as an argument (cache_path) by the caller from an environment variable or static path — not derived from the cache key. There are no os.path.join calls that incorporate the cache key. No path traversal risk exists.
exploit_scenario: N/A — no path traversal vector identified.

question: Q4
title: Embedding cache is NOT rolled back if storage write fails — phantom cache entries persist
finding: In service.py (demo path), embeddings are written to the cache file (save_cache) before the recall/composite scoring returns. In routes/memory_v1.py, the write route calls _generate_embedding() at Step 4, then adapter.upsert() at Step 7, then _persist_memory() at Step 8. If upsert() or _persist_memory() raises, the broad except block raises HTTP 500. The embedding generated at Step 4 is held in the local variable 'embedding' and is NOT written to any cache in the v1 route (the v1 route uses a local hash-based _generate_embedding() stub, not the embed_cache module). So for the v1 production path there is no cache entry to roll back. However, if a real embedding provider is wired in (as the comment 'In production, use actual embedding provider' implies), any caching layer used by that provider would not be invalidated on write failure. The structural pattern — embed then store, with no rollback path — means any future caching integration inherits this phantom-entry risk.
exact_lines:
# Step 4: Generate embedding
# Note: In production, use embedding provider
embedding = await _generate_embedding(request.memory.content)

# Step 7: Upsert to vector DB
await adapter.upsert(vector_id, embedding, metadata)

# Step 8: Persist to document store (SQLite)
await _persist_memory(...)

...
except Exception as e:
    logger.exception("v1.memory.write.error", ...)
    raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail="Internal error")
exploit_scenario: When a production embedding provider with its own JSONL cache is integrated (as the code comments anticipate), a partial failure after embed() but before persist will leave a stale embedding cached for a memory that was never admitted. On retry, the cached embedding is served without re-embedding — meaning the memory is stored with an embedding that predates any content that may have changed between attempts.

question: Q6
title: Canonicalizer hashes whatever it is given — no field-level validation before hashing
finding: The Canonicalizer class in canonicalizer.py performs only structural normalization (Unicode NFC, float rounding, key sorting, NaN/Inf rejection) before hashing. It does not validate field values against any policy (no forbidden-key check, no content firewall, no omega rule check). The canonical_hash() convenience function calls to_json() which calls canonicalize() which calls _canonicalize_value() — purely structural transformations. In the write route, _compute_content_hash() (a separate function, not using the Canonicalizer module) directly builds a dict from request fields and hashes it with SHA-256 before validation runs in the module-level validators.py sense. Critically, the canonicalizer.py module and the v1 write path's _compute_content_hash() are distinct code paths — the write route does NOT use the Canonicalizer class at all. This creates two separate canonicalization implementations with no cross-validation.
exploit_scenario: Two canonicalization implementations exist for the same conceptual purpose. If the validators.py path (which does run content checks) were ever connected to the Canonicalizer module, the hash would cover pre-validation data. As-is, the risk is drift: a developer adds content mutation in the Canonicalizer (e.g., stripping a field) and the idempotency hash no longer matches what was actually validated.

question: Q9
title: Content hash truncated to 32 hex chars (128 bits) — collision risk elevated, truncation applied consistently
finding: In _compute_content_hash(), the full SHA-256 hex digest (64 chars, 256 bits) is computed, then truncated to 32 characters (128 bits) at both storage and lookup. The truncation is applied consistently: _persist_memory() stores content_hash[:32] and _check_idempotency() queries with content_hash[:32]. So the system is internally consistent — no mismatch between insert and lookup. However, storing only 128 bits of a SHA-256 hash reduces collision resistance from ~2^128 to ~2^64 in a birthday-attack scenario (birthday bound for 128-bit space is ~2^64 operations). For an idempotency check (not a security-critical hash), this is borderline acceptable — but the code is using it to gate writes (a collision would cause a legitimate new write to return an existing memory ID as if the content were a duplicate). The rationale comment says 'truncated to match column size' — this suggests a schema constraint drove the truncation rather than a deliberate security decision.
exact_lines:
# _check_idempotency:
cursor = await db.execute(
    """
    SELECT memory_id, receipt_hash, created_at
    FROM v1_memories WHERE content_hash = ?
    LIMIT 1
    """,
    (content_hash[:32],)  # Match the stored hash length
)

# _persist_memory:
# Store content hash for idempotency (truncated to match column size)
content_hash[:32],
exploit_scenario: An attacker who can craft content with a 128-bit SHA-256 prefix collision (2^64 expected attempts — feasible with significant resources) can cause a legitimate write to be rejected as a duplicate of an existing memory, or cause their content to be returned as a previously admitted memory in idempotent responses. At scale (large corpus), natural birthday collisions at 128 bits become non-negligible.

question: Q10
title: All database queries use parameterized queries — no SQL injection vulnerability found
finding: Every SQL operation in the write and recall routes uses aiosqlite's parameterized query interface (db.execute with a tuple of parameters). _check_idempotency passes (content_hash[:32],) as a parameter tuple. _persist_memory passes all eleven fields as a parameter tuple. _get_memory_summary passes (memory_id,) as a parameter tuple. In all cases user-supplied data is passed as bound parameters, never interpolated into the SQL string via f-strings or % formatting. The queries themselves are string literals. No SQL injection vector was identified in the code provided.
exact_lines:
# _check_idempotency:
cursor = await db.execute(
    """
    SELECT memory_id, receipt_hash, created_at
    FROM v1_memories WHERE content_hash = ?
    LIMIT 1
    """,
    (content_hash[:32],)
)

# _persist_memory:
await db.execute(
    """
    INSERT INTO v1_memories (
        memory_id, memory_class, jurisdiction, content_hash, ...
    ) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
    """,
    (memory_id, request.memory.memory_class.value, ...)
)

# _get_memory_summary:
cursor = await db.execute(
    "SELECT content_hash FROM v1_memories WHERE memory_id = ?",
    (memory_id,),
)
exploit_scenario: N/A — parameterized queries are used consistently throughout.

question: Q7

Must re-test after model version upgrades — activation patterns and failure modes are model-version-specific.' Apply the same model version recording recommendation as MA-22.

title: upsert() never calls _validate_metadata_at_boundary — constitutional invariant silently violated
failure_mode: BaseVectorAdapter._validate_metadata_at_boundary() is declared with the comment 'CONSTITUTIONAL REQUIREMENT: Must be called before ANY upsert' (base.py line 579). QdrantAdapter.upsert() calls _metadata_to_payload() and then directly calls self._client.upsert() without ever invoking _validate_metadata_at_boundary(). Any caller-supplied VectorMetadata with forbidden authority fields (whatever validate_metadata_keys() checks) is persisted to the vector store without the fail-closed rejection the contract promises. Because the abstract base does not call the validator itself before delegating, and because the method is not abstract, concrete subclasses can — and here do — silently skip the check. This is a complete bypass of the stated security gate for all writes through QdrantAdapter.

title: Unvalidated jurisdiction value flows directly into Qdrant FieldCondition filter
failure_mode: filters.jurisdiction is passed verbatim as MatchValue(value=filters.jurisdiction) with no type check, length bound, or allowlist validation. If the jurisdiction value originates from a caller-controlled source (request body, URL parameter, etc.) and the Qdrant client or server interprets certain value shapes specially, an attacker can craft a jurisdiction string that broadens or nullifies the filter condition. More concretely: if jurisdiction is set to a value that matches every stored point (e.g. an empty string that acts as a wildcard in some Qdrant versions, or a value matching a different tenant's namespace), cross-tenant data leaks without any server-side guard stopping the query.

title: get_by_memory_id() accepts unvalidated memory_id in scroll filter — no format or length check
failure_mode: memory_id is taken directly from the caller and embedded as MatchValue(value=memory_id) in a Qdrant scroll filter. There is no validation that memory_id conforms to an expected format (UUID, bounded length, character set). An oversized or specially crafted memory_id string is sent to the Qdrant server as-is. Beyond filter manipulation, an unbounded string can cause excessive memory allocation in the client serialisation layer or trigger unexpected server-side behaviour. Additionally, because the scroll is limited=1 but there is no jurisdiction or class constraint in this query, a valid memory_id belonging to a different tenant is fully retrievable if the caller knows or guesses it.

title: Health check exposes internal collection topology in success path
failure_mode: On a successful health check the method returns a HealthStatus.details dict containing 'collection' (the internal collection name), 'vectors_count', 'points_count', and 'status' (collection status enum). If the health endpoint is reachable by any authenticated caller — or is unauthenticated as is typical for health endpoints — this discloses the exact collection name (enabling targeted Qdrant API calls if credentials are obtained), the total number of stored memory records across all tenants, and the collection operational status. The failure path at line 324 also sets error=str(e), which may include the Qdrant server URL, collection name, or TCP connection details depending on which exception is raised.

title: update_lifecycle() is a read-modify-write without optimistic locking — TOCTOU race condition
failure_mode: The method retrieves the current point at line 339-344, validates the transition at line 355, then sets the payload at line 358-362. Between the retrieve and the set_payload there is no version field, ETag, or conditional update. Two concurrent callers with the same id — one transitioning ACTIVE→TOMBSTONED and one transitioning ACTIVE→PRUNED — will both read ACTIVE, both pass validation, and then race to write. The second write silently overwrites the first without any conflict detection, leaving the audit trail in an inconsistent state. For a governed memory service this is a data-integrity violation: the lifecycle history no longer reflects what actually happened.

title: delete() swallows all exceptions and always returns True on non-exception path — silent audit failure
failure_mode: The Qdrant delete API does not raise an exception when asked to delete a non-existent point; it silently succeeds. The method therefore returns True for both successful deletions and deletions of IDs that never existed, making it impossible for callers to distinguish a real deletion from a no-op. The broad except Exception at line 295 additionally swallows any Qdrant connectivity failure after a partial operation. For audit trail purposes the system believes a delete occurred when it may not have, and there is no mechanism to detect or retry genuine failures.

title: delete() and update_lifecycle() warning logs include the raw caller-supplied id — potential log injection
lines: 296 (delete), 370 (update_lifecycle)
failure_mode: logger.warning(f'Delete failed for {id}: {e}') and logger.warning(f'Lifecycle update failed for {id}: {e}') interpolate the raw caller-supplied id string directly into log output with no sanitisation. If id contains newline characters, ANSI escape sequences, or log-forging payloads (e.g. '\nERROR: admin login from 1.2.3.4'), an attacker with write access can inject false log entries, corrupt structured log parsers, or — in environments where log output is rendered — trigger terminal escape sequence attacks. The exception message e may additionally contain Qdrant internals (connection strings, collection names, point IDs).

title: No TLS enforcement or API key requirement on QdrantClient construction
failure_mode: self._client = QdrantClient(url=url, api_key=api_key) where api_key defaults to None and url defaults to 'http://localhost:6333'. No code enforces that url uses the https:// scheme or that api_key is non-None before the client is constructed. In a deployment where the caller forgets to supply these values, the adapter silently connects over plaintext HTTP with no authentication. All memory vectors and metadata — including jurisdiction, provenance, and lifecycle state — transit in cleartext. No warning is logged when api_key is None.

title: No retry logic, backoff, or circuit breaker on any Qdrant operation
failure_mode: Every call to self._client.* is made once with no retry wrapper. A transient Qdrant network hiccup causes the entire upsert, query, or lifecycle update to fail with an exception propagated to the caller or silently swallowed (delete, update_lifecycle). There is no exponential backoff, jitter, or circuit breaker. Under sustained instability this results in: (a) write loss — upsert failures are not retried so memories are silently dropped; (b) query failures surfaced to the application; (c) resource exhaustion if callers implement their own tight retry loops against a failing Qdrant instance.

title: _payload_to_metadata() performs unguarded dict key access — KeyError on malformed stored payload
failure_mode: Fields memory_id, memory_class, jurisdiction, provenance_ref, omega_rule_ref, and admission_receipt_hash are accessed with direct dict subscript (payload["memory_id"] etc.) rather than .get(). If any of these fields is absent from a stored Qdrant payload — due to a schema migration, a bug in a previous write path, or direct Qdrant manipulation — _payload_to_metadata() raises an unhandled KeyError. This bubbles through _query_raw(), _query_with_native_filter(), and get_by_memory_id() as an unhandled exception, crashing the query rather than returning a graceful error or skipping the malformed record. An attacker who can write directly to Qdrant can selectively corrupt payloads to trigger denial-of-service on query paths.

title: Concurrent upserts for the same logical ID are not idempotent under Qdrant's eventual consistency
failure_mode: Qdrant's upsert is atomic per point, but two concurrent upserts for the same point_id (derived deterministically from id) with different embeddings or metadata will produce a last-write-wins outcome with no conflict notification. The caller has no way to detect that their write was overwritten. For a governed memory service where admission_receipt_hash and provenance_ref must correspond to the stored embedding, a race between an update and a re-ingestion of the same memory ID can leave the hash inconsistent with the vector, silently breaking integrity guarantees.

title: _query_raw() fetches k * pre_filter_multiplier results without an upper bound — potential resource exhaustion
failure_mode: The limit passed to Qdrant is k * pre_filter_multiplier where k and pre_filter_multiplier are both caller-controlled (pre_filter_multiplier has a default of 5 but is an exposed parameter). There is no maximum cap. If a caller requests k=10000 with a multiplier of 5, the adapter requests 50,000 points from Qdrant in a single call. The response is held in memory as a list before any filtering. This enables memory exhaustion in the adapter process and potentially in the Qdrant server, and imposes unbounded latency on query responses.

title: query() uses assert for constitutional invariant enforcement — disabled by Python optimisation flag
failure_mode: assert result.authoritative is False, 'CONSTITUTIONAL VIOLATION: ...' is the sole enforcement point for the authoritative=False invariant on all query results. Python's assert statements are compiled out when the interpreter is run with the -O (optimise) flag, which is standard practice in many production deployments and Docker base images (PYTHONOPTIMIZE=1). When asserts are disabled, the constitutional invariant is not enforced: any VectorResult with authoritative=True (injected via a Qdrant payload manipulation, a buggy adapter, or a corrupt read) passes through silently. This is stated as a 'defense in depth' check but is the only programmatic enforcement; if it is silently disabled the defense collapses entirely.

title: _validate_metadata_at_boundary() is a non-abstract method with no call in the concrete write path — implementers can skip it
failure_mode: The method is defined as a concrete (non-abstract) instance method on BaseVectorAdapter. Because it is not abstract, Python does not require subclasses to call it. The docstring labels it 'CONSTITUTIONAL REQUIREMENT: Must be called before ANY upsert' but nothing in the class hierarchy enforces this: the abstract upsert() signature (lines 623-630) contains no super() call or decorator that wraps the validation. QdrantAdapter.upsert() already demonstrates this failure — it never calls the method. Any future adapter will have the same silent bypass unless the author reads the docstring carefully. The fix requires either making upsert() non-abstract with a template-method pattern that calls validation before delegating, or raising NotImplementedError in a __init_subclass__ hook.

title: _query_with_native_filter() has a default implementation that silently degrades to post-filtering
failure_mode: The base class provides a default implementation of _query_with_native_filter() that ignores the native-filter path and falls back to _query_raw() + _apply_filters(). A subclass that sets _supports_native_filtering() to return True but forgets to override _query_with_native_filter() will silently use the post-filter fallback: fetching a large over-sample (k*5) and filtering in Python. The caller has no indication this is happening. More critically, post-filtering in base.py applies jurisdiction and class filters in Python, but the jurisdiction filter is only applied if filters.jurisdiction is truthy — an empty string jurisdiction would pass through unfiltered, potentially leaking cross-tenant records. The _query_raw() over-fetch combined with post-filtering also lacks the upper bound problem noted in the qdrant.py finding above.

title: get_by_memory_id() is absent from the abstract contract — implementations are optional and undiscoverable
lines: 623-657 (abstract method block); qdrant.py lines 373-402
failure_mode: get_by_memory_id() is implemented in QdrantAdapter but is not declared in BaseVectorAdapter or the VectorAdapter protocol. Callers that depend on this method are coupled to the concrete QdrantAdapter type. Any alternative adapter (in-memory, Pinecone, Weaviate) silently lacks the method without any static or runtime indication. If a caller type-annotated to BaseVectorAdapter or VectorAdapter calls get_by_memory_id(), they get an AttributeError at runtime with no early-warning from the type system. For a governed memory service where audit lookups by memory_id may be required, this is a contract gap.

title: update_lifecycle() abstract docstring does not specify the return type contract for non-existent IDs
failure_mode: The abstract docstring says implementations should '1. Get current state, 2. Validate transition, 3. Update state' but does not specify whether a False return means 'ID not found' or 'operation failed'. QdrantAdapter returns False for both cases (lines 346-347 for not-found, line 371 for exception). Callers cannot distinguish a governance-relevant event (attempted lifecycle transition on a non-existent memory) from a transient infrastructure failure, preventing correct audit logging and retry logic.

title: validate_memory_recall() applies NO content-level firewall to the query field
finding: validate_memory_recall() runs only schema validation (Gate 1: Pydantic). There is no content firewall scan on the `query` field, no forbidden-key scan, and no entropy/transcript detection. The query string (up to Pydantic's min_length=1, no max_length is set on MemoryRecallRequest.query) is passed directly to recall_memories() and echoed back verbatim in the MemoryRecallResponse.query field. The query string is also passed to embed_provider.embed() if vector recall is used — which can be a vector database query. A sufficiently large or malformed query could abuse the embedding pipeline or exfiltrate data through query logging.
exact_lines:
def validate_memory_recall(payload: Dict[str, Any]) -> MemoryRecallRequest:
    # Gate 1: Schema validation
    try:
        request = MemoryRecallRequest(**payload)
    ...
    # Gate 2: Budget bounds are enforced by schema (max_items <= 100, max_tokens <= 8000)
    # Gate 3: Class allowlist validation is enforced by schema (enum validation)
    return request
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends a recall request with `query` containing a multi-kilobyte string of raw transcript text. No firewall check fires. The query is echoed back in the response (MemoryRecallResponse.query field) and sent to the embedding provider without sanitization. With no max_length on the query field in the schema, this also opens a DoS vector against the embedding pipeline.

title: Storage errors in create_memory() propagate as generic MEM_SCHEMA_INVALID — swallows DB constraint violations
finding: In memory_write_internal(), the outer except-block catches all non-MemoryError exceptions and re-wraps them as MemoryErrorCode.MEM_SCHEMA_INVALID with a truncated message. In the main write route, a generic Exception re-raises as HTTPException(500). In create_memory() itself, no exception handling is present — DB constraint errors (e.g., UNIQUE constraint on `id`) will propagate up and be caught by the route's generic handler. The idempotency check runs a SELECT before the INSERT, but between the SELECT and INSERT there is a race window (TOCTOU): two concurrent requests with the same ID can both pass the idempotency check (SELECT returns no row for both) and then one INSERT succeeds while the other raises a DB UNIQUE constraint error that is swallowed as a 500 rather than a 409 Conflict.
exploit_scenario: Two concurrent writes with the same memory ID arrive simultaneously. Both pass the idempotency SELECT (no existing row found). Both proceed to INSERT. One succeeds; the other gets a DB UNIQUE constraint error, which is re-wrapped as MEM_SCHEMA_INVALID (400) instead of MEM_ID_CONFLICT (409). The client cannot distinguish a schema error from a race-condition conflict.

title: MaintenanceAuthority checks forbidden types by exact set membership — NOT bypassable by substring, but whitespace-padded strings would pass the FORBIDDEN set and fail the ALLOWED set, landing in S3MaintenanceHalt (correct behavior)
finding: FORBIDDEN_MAINTENANCE_AUTHORITY_TYPES and MAINTENANCE_AUTHORITY_TYPES are both frozensets of lowercase exact strings. The check in MaintenanceAuthority.__post_init__() uses `in` operator against the frozenset, which is exact-match only. A caller sending `authority_type = ' runtime'` (with leading space) would NOT match the forbidden set, but would also NOT match the allowed set, causing the second check (`not in MAINTENANCE_AUTHORITY_TYPES`) to raise S3MaintenanceHalt with the 'Invalid authority type' message. The outcome is still a halt (correct behavior), but the violation code is 'Invalid' rather than 'Forbidden', which could confuse audit log analysis. No bypass is possible through this mechanism.
exact_lines:
MAINTENANCE_AUTHORITY_TYPES: FrozenSet[str] = frozenset(["human", "system"])
FORBIDDEN_MAINTENANCE_AUTHORITY_TYPES: FrozenSet[str] = frozenset([
    "runtime",
    "replay",
    "consolidation",
])
...
if self.authority_type in FORBIDDEN_MAINTENANCE_AUTHORITY_TYPES:
    raise S3MaintenanceHalt(...)
if self.authority_type not in MAINTENANCE_AUTHORITY_TYPES:
    raise S3MaintenanceHalt(...)
exploit_scenario: Not exploitable. Any value not in MAINTENANCE_AUTHORITY_TYPES halts regardless of whether it matches FORBIDDEN_MAINTENANCE_AUTHORITY_TYPES.

title: emit_audit() accepts caller-supplied payload dict with no schema validation — raw caller data emitted without sanitization
finding: The emit_audit() convenience function and AppendOnlyAuditSink.emit() accept a `payload: Dict[str, Any]` with no schema validation. The payload is JSON-serialized and hashed, but not validated against any schema. Any caller can supply arbitrary keys and values. The payload is hashed (not stored in the AuditEntry itself — only the hash is stored), so audit entries cannot directly leak raw content. However, the payload is passed to json.dumps() without size limits, meaning a caller could supply a very large payload dict to cause resource exhaustion in the audit emission path. The maintenance admission pipeline supplies structured payloads, so in practice the current callers are benign — but there is no enforcement of payload structure.
exact_lines:
def emit_audit(
    run_id: str,
    event_type: AuditEventType,
    layer: AuditLayer,
    payload: Dict[str, Any],
    **kwargs,
) -> AuditEntry:
    ...
    payload_json = json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True, separators=(',', ':'))
    payload_hash = hashlib.sha256(payload_json.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()
exploit_scenario: An internal caller (or future code path) passes a very large or deeply nested payload dict. json.dumps() on a deeply nested structure can be slow or exhaust memory. No size cap is enforced.

title: validate_memory_recall() does NOT apply content firewall to query field — confirmed gap (see V2-2), no pre-computed embedding injection possible via public API
finding: The v2 recall route does not accept a pre-computed embedding from the caller. The embedding is computed server-side by _get_recall_provider() using the validated query string. There is no field in MemoryRecallRequest for caller-supplied embeddings. The recall path enforces class constraints via the Pydantic schema (allowed_classes is a required List[MemoryClass] with min_items=1). Tombstoned items are excluded at the SQL layer (summary != '[TOMBSTONED]'). The query field has no max_length bound in the schema (only min_length=1), which is a gap (see V2-2). No embedding injection vulnerability exists in the current schema.
exact_lines:
query: str = Field(..., min_length=1, description="Natural language query")
allowed_classes: List[MemoryClass] = Field(
    ...,
    min_items=1,
    description="Explicit allowlist of classes to query"
)
exploit_scenario: N/A for embedding injection. Query length unbounded (see V2-2).

title: recall_memories() echoes request.query verbatim into MemoryRecallResponse — content reflection vector
finding: The MemoryRecallResponse model includes a `query` field that stores the original query string. In recall_memories(), this is populated directly from `request.query`. Since validate_memory_recall() applies no content firewall to the query, and the query is echoed back in the response, an attacker can use the recall endpoint as a content reflection mechanism. If the response is logged, forwarded, or displayed in a UI, the raw query (potentially containing injected content) is replicated. The response is also returned to the caller verbatim.
exact_lines:
return MemoryRecallResponse(
    query=request.query,
    items=items,
    ...)
exploit_scenario: A caller sends a recall request with `query` containing a prompt injection payload. The response echoes the query back. If the response is processed by another LLM or logged in a system that renders the query, the injected content is reflected and potentially executed.

title: MemoryWriteRequest.id field is unbounded string (min_length=1, no max_length)
finding: The `id` field in MemoryWriteRequest is defined as `str = Field(..., min_length=1, description=...)` with no max_length. The id is used as a database primary key, in idempotency SELECT/INSERT queries (parameterized, so no SQL injection), and in error messages. However, an arbitrarily long id (e.g., 1MB string) would be stored in the database and returned in responses. Combined with the fact that content_hash computation serializes the id as part of the canonical JSON, a very long id could cause performance issues in the hashing step. There is no format validation (no UUID check, no character allowlist) — any string is accepted.
exact_lines: id: str = Field(..., min_length=1, description="Deterministic or content-hash ID")
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends a write request with a 1MB id string. The id is stored in the database, used in hash computation (CPU cost), and returned in error messages if conflicts arise. Repeated with many unique ids, this is a storage DoS.

title: MemoryRecallRequest.query has no max_length bound — unbounded string flows to embedding provider
finding: MemoryRecallRequest.query is defined as `str = Field(..., min_length=1, ...)` with no max_length. This string is passed to embed_provider.embed([request.query]) when vector recall is used, and to the FTS query. Embedding providers typically have token limits (e.g., 8192 tokens for text-embedding-3-small). Sending a query exceeding the provider's limit will either cause a provider error (which is caught by the fallback path in recall_memories — the vector reranking failure degrades to lexical-only) or truncation without the caller being informed. A large query string is also echoed in the response (see V2-15).
exact_lines: query: str = Field(..., min_length=1, description="Natural language query")
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends a recall request with a 100KB query. The embedding provider receives the full string, potentially causing a timeout or error. The fallback silently degrades to lexical-only. The 100KB string is echoed in the response. Logging systems that capture the full response body store the 100KB string.
